content: Adam Block is the former CRO at Motive, a sales org with 3,000+ reps. Here's how they successfully moved up-market to grow their enterprise segment... We recorded this right before Adam announced his departure from Motive. Three things he said hit hard. And they contradict the instinct of most sales leaders I talk to. ✅ 1. Cut book sizes. Always. Adam: "I don't recall a time that I've ever reduced book sizes and didn't see productivity go up." The instinct as a rep, and as a sales leader looking at TAM, is more accounts equals more opportunity. The opposite is true at enterprise. Sometimes it makes sense for a strategic AE to have 1-3 accounts. With entire teams supporting them. If your enterprise AEs are still carrying 50+ accounts, try cutting their books by a quarter to a half. Watch what happens. As a rep, disqualify a big chunk of your accounts. ✅ 2. Kill the three-pod model. Motive used to run new logo, expansion sales, and CSM as three separate roles. The classic pod. They collapsed it. Why: too many cooks, no single owner with a net number...etc. The fix. One account manager owns the customer forever (or one new-logo AE keeps the account on the largest deals). They become the quarterback. They pull in product, support, finance, or supply chain when needed. But there's one name on the account. Result: better retention AND more expansion revenue. Customers stopped getting passed around. If you're running the three-pod model and your expansion number isn't there, this might be your fix. ✅ 3. The 90-second test. Adam was a part of every new hire class. His ask before any rep touched a single account: Explain who Motive is, what we do, how we do it better, the impact, and where we've done it. In 90 to 120 seconds. To your parent. To a neighbor. To anyone. If a new SDR or AE can't do that, they can't have a real conversation with a buyer. Period. ~~~ If you're org is moving up-market, or struggling to do so, you'll love this episode. We talked about: 1) Adam's journey from VP of Sales to CRO at Motive 2) How Motive moved from down-market to enterprise in 3 years 3) Proven tactics for moving up-market Watch the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/gRBCbpFw

content: We recently helped 300+ AEs at iHeartMedia increase self-sourced meetings by 40%+ And they were not starting at zero. H2 pipeline is alarmingly thin for many sales orgs right now. And you might be getting a lot of resistance from AEs on doing outbound: "This is marketing's job." "This is our BDR's job." "I don't have time for PG, I'm working deals." But let's face it: the whole "let's cut AI agents loose to find us leads" hasn't been working for most sales orgs. Blame it on the org. Blame it on the tool. I don't care. The need for AEs to self-source pipeline isn't going away. Self-sourcing is an ATTITUDE. It either exists in your sales culture or it doesn't. Here's what we're seeing working right now: ✅ 1) Train the AI Signing your team up for Claude accounts isn't good enough. You need examples of great outbound and process loaded into your LLM as skills. You have to train the AI for it to work well. And you need a ton of examples of what good looks like. ✅ 2) Up-skill the AEs as much as you do the BDRs Train on what's working in 2026. Spend time on the phones, email, LinkedIn, etc. AEs need role-playing practice, weekly coaching, etc. ✅ 3) Enable front-line leaders Too often, I see groups of front-line managers who don't know how to outbound. How could you possibly expect an AE to prioritize PG when you don't know how to teach it? Enable front-line leaders on how to incorporate PG into 1on1s, how to develop/coach reps, and how to outbound themselves. ✅ 4) Rub shoulders. Front-line managers get in the pit with their AEs every week. Make calls together into tier-1 accounts and their toughest-to-reach prospects. ✅ 5) Arm AEs with the right tools Too often, I see a sales org with BDRs using a sales engagement platform, but the AEs have to do outbound manually with zero tooling. Give AEs the same access to sales engagement tools as you do for your SDRs & BDRs. You have ZERO accountability if you can’t measure outbound activity. We love Orum 🥇's* dialing solution. ✅ 6) Top-down involvement CRO and VP of Sales should participate in outbound efforts by giving public shout-outs in Slack for reps landing meetings through outbound. They should celebrate effort, share stories, and get the team pumped up. ✅ 7) Data Get best-in-class email and phone data. We love TitanX* for that. You can’t expect AEs to make dials without access to solid email addresses & mobile numbers. ~~~ Creating a PG culture with AEs doesn't happen overnight. But hitting your number in 2026/27 won't be possible without it. Start now. What would you add to this list? *Our partners, not sponsors

content: "I send 10,000+ cold emails a day, but I have to say: NOBODY should cold call." I could not believe my eyes. Had to comment on this post. 🍀Ian Hill's response really hit me..."Have we already come full circle that we're just spray and praying again?" He nailed it. At the end of the day, all this "fully automate outbound with this Claude workflow" nonsense is exactly that. It's a re-packaged version of spraying and praying. It's incrementally "better" than dropping the same message into a sequencer and sending it. But not by much. - It's still noise - If you're sending thousands per day, the quality isn't great - It p*sses off our buyers It's the equivalent of littering in the park and not giving a f*ck about how it impacts other people. I'm 100% bullish on the phones and in-person right now because it's the least crowded way to get attention. Execs get maybe a dozen cold calls per day if they're bigwigs. No one's reaching out to meet them in person. Nobody's stopping by their office. Please don't fall for this nonsense. Pick up the phone and get after it.

content: If I was a BDR or AE trying to break into my dream account, here's exactly what I'd do: I'd come up with an offer that made the buyer feel IRRESPONSIBLE for not taking the meeting with me. And I'd do this with a killer offer. This is the outbound datapoint everyone should be building their 2026/27 strategy around. Offers increase email reply rates by up to 28% (Gong) Offers are NOT: • A free demo • A gift card • A “meet and greet” They're tangible gives. Like a cart audit let's say if you sell an ecommerce solution. Dig through the checkout process and analyze: • Missed upsell opportunities • Broken processes • Lack of cart abandon follow up etc Here's how you can use offers to increase the effectiveness of your cold outreach: ✅ Offer #1 - Pitch The Blind Date Position who the buyer will be meeting with. I'd hype up the AE, sales engineer, or myself depending on my industry experience. Example: A client of ours sells an automated welding solution Example email CTA: "Our welding solutions engineer, Paul, is working with Hitch manufacturers like CURT to help overcome the welder shortage and produce consistent, high-quality welds. Interested in a quick intro?" ✅ Offer #2 - 1:Many Offers High-quality, reusable insights that feel tailored. Competitive benchmarks, industry research, or best practice guides. Example: We have a client that sells to a clinical ops solution. They surveyed dozens of clients in key areas around clinical trials. Example email CTA: "We ran a benchmarking analysis with a few of your peers like Ultromics and MEDRhythms to find avg. study start times, drop-out rates, insights on how they shorten build times. Interested in seeing how you stack up?" ✅ Offer #3 - 1:1 Offers These are custom-tailored experiences or resources created specifically for the prospect. This works best at the enterprise & strategic levels. Examples: - A cyber risk analysis - A benchmarking analysis - A workshop - A personalized audit of a website checkout flow. - Visiting and experiencing the brand firsthand, then sharing insights. - Offering free data, licenses, or pilots. Example: An EdTech rep would use language like this when reaching out to school districts "We compiled a report of how WCUS stacks up against the top schools in WA & OR. Free next week to run through the report together? Tom P.S. If nothing else, you’ll see how other PNW school districts are minimizing student inequity and achievement gaps to increase student readiness and graduation rates" ~~~ Offers are the future of outbound. It'll help your emails, calls, and DMs cut through the noise. What are you giving the buyer, that even if they decided not to buy your solution, makes the time they spend with you worthwhile?

content: If I were looking to hire my next all-star sales rep, here's exactly what I'd focus on... We've worked with 250+ sales teams and over 10,000 reps in the last 5-6 years. Teams as large as Shopify, Zoom, Rippling, etc. And teams with 3-5 reps rolling out an outbound motion for the first time. 20,000+ reps. The best reps fit into one very specific archetype: 1. The grinder 2. The procrastinator 3. The cyborg 4. The owner A grinder never misses their activity target. They muscle their way through pipegen. They're in the top 25%, but never the top 10%. The procrastinator only builds pipeline at the beginning of every quarter...when they don't have any. They're last. The cyborg leverages every AI tool imaginable. They're a modern day version of the grinder, but they never develop a BIG book of business because their buyers don't respond to AI-written emails. And they don't like to pick up the phone. The best archetype we've seen is the OWNER. This rep acts as a CEO of their territory. They: ✅ 1) Disqualify. Ruthlessly disqualify opportunities unworthy of their energy, time, and attention. ✅ 2) Offer. Consultative and make strategic “gives” to buyers early and often to earn their time. ✅ 3) Provoke. Engage buyers with a strong point of view on their problem that compels action now. And they book 4.3x more meetings than their avg. performing peers. They know that in a world full of AI, they must be Too Good To Ignore. The owner is the archetype you should be hiring for in your next best rep. Agree or disagree?

content: "Cold outbound" is only ONE flavor of "pipegen." It's the coldest, least effective way to engage buyers. I'm not saying it doesn't work. We've built out our entire business at Outbound Squad by helping companies like Shopify, Rippling, Zoom, etc crush at it. But it's the very last thing you should do. Mitch Thomas runs enablement at Brooksource for 180+ large enterprise AEs with ZERO BDR support. They self-source all of their pipeline. Do they make cold calls? Yes. But it's not the only way their very best reps source pipeline: 1) Referrals 2) Community events & philanthropy 3) Industry associations 4) Tradeshows & conferences 5) Following champions to new businesses 6) Asking for introductions 7) In-person drop bys Phone, email, and LinkedIn are just tools. You can choose to have ice cold conversations, or you can "warm them up" by looking for a better path in. When we worked with Brooksource, they were already converting 22% of their outbound calls into qualified meetings. After we worked together, it was 35%+. I've never seen it higher with any other client. I'd love to take more credit for it, but a big part was calling into leads that weren't completely cold. If there's one way to virtually guarantee that you land more meetings, it's do just that. In this interview, we talked about: - How Brooksource’s 180+ AEs self-source pipeline without BDRs - Building a world-class onboarding process - How enablement builds strong partnership with sales Watch the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/eTuQxV4T

content: 250+ sales teams. 10,000+ reps. Here’s the best cold call opener… 👇 We’ve studied hundreds of thousands of cold calls across 250+ sales teams in the last 6 years. And there’s one thing we can tell you for sure: The best reps all use different openers. That’s right. There’s no one opener. Or handful of openers that work the best. BUT…here’s what every great opener that led to a meeting had in common. The opener has three parts: ✅ Part 1) A greeting “Hi [first name], it’s [your first name…” They answer the first question every prospect has: who are you? ✅ Part 2) The three Ts Here’s where it starts to get interesting. Each of the openers leverages one of what we call the “three Ts”: Trigger: Make it timely by finding signals/triggers Tension: Make it relevant by sharing a problem Trust: Make it credible by leveraging social proof Sometimes they include all three. But every time, they include at least one. “Hi [first name], it’s [your first name…saw you’re hiring welders…” That’s an example of leveraging a trigger. ✅ Part 3) Permission Gong data from 300k+ cold calls supports the same. Permission-based openers outperform any other opener by nearly 5x. “Hi [first name], it’s [your first name…saw you’re hiring welders…have a minute for me to share why I called you specifically?” A simple permission-based question. ~~~ Now here’s what it looks like when you start to get creative: Greeting + Tension + Permission: “Hi Dave, was calling about the welder shortage everyone’s dealing with right now. Got a minute for me to share more?” Greeting + Trigger + Tension + Trust + Permission: “Hi Kate, noticed you’re hiring MIG welders, not sure if you’re running into challenges automating some of those low volume welds folks like Caterpillar have had. Got a min? Promise to be brief.” Greeting + Trust + Permission: “Hi Tom, we work with several trailer hitch manufacturers like XYZ. It’s Jason with ABC COMPANY. Heard our name tossed around?” (h/t to Armand Farrokh) ~~~ The best cold call openers are the ones you create. Trigger, tension, trust is just the ingredients. You make your own recipe. Want more? I was just featured in HubSpot’s most recent The Science of Scaling newsletter. Check it out here: https://lnkd.in/gFHWKk6p

content: Not all humans are fit for outbound Tara DiCristo-Schmitt is the CRO at Houzz. She also spent 5 years as a VP of Sales at HubSpot and started her sales career at ADP. Tara has a hot take I can't stop thinking about (and couldn’t agree with more). Stop trying to convert your inbound team to an outbound team. This is the request we get more than any other. "We've been an inbound shop. Pipeline's down. We need to flip the team." Keep your inbound team intact. Or in the AI era, reduce headcount. That's revenue you can depend on. Don't break the thing that works. For outbound, hire net new. Build the army separately, with people who actually want to do the work. You can teach skill. You cannot teach will. A generation of reps joined inbound shops because they were promised they wouldn't have to cold call. You can run the best enablement program in the world, and most of them still won't cut it. Every "we tried mandatory call blocks and it didn't work" story starts with a team hired for one motion and asked to do another. In this episode, we talked about: ✅ Tara's journey from ADP rep (and one of the first women sales leaders) to HubSpot to CRO at Houzz ✅ Why retraining inbound reps to outbound is a trap ✅ The director days ritual that exposes coaching gaps ✅ Her weekly rhythm as a CRO And much, much more Watch the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/eUyC3nXd

content: I disagree that the first line of a cold email is to get them to read the second line. That's not how most people actually read written content. Google, Nielsen, CMSWire, EyeQuant...they've all done studies over the years. And the conclusion is this: - People read in either an F shape - OR people read just the left-hand side of the page - 2/3 of people skip the intro and jump straight to the end - The middle body is the most overlooked part of the content When I closely pay attention to my own habits, I open the email, read the first line, then immediately skip to the CTA to see what the ask is. So what's the objective of the first line? Two parts: 1) Drive the open 2) Build urgency The first line of the email shows up in the preview text before the prospect ever opens it. To drive opens, it's just as important as the subject line. Next, the first line should build urgency by talking about something hyper-relevant to the buyer. Something going on with their business, a challenge across their industry, a relevant competitor, etc Here are three options, using an HR solution as an example: ✅ Trigger opener Open with a trigger that helps answer the "why now?" question for the buyer. "Saw you just opened locations in Italy and France..." ✅ Tension Open with a problem you suspect they might have, or that's prevalent across their industry. "International hiring can create a ton of compliance challenges that slows down hiring." ✅ Trust Open with a social proof statement. "Not sure if you're running into this, but ABC COMPANY ran into tons of compliance challenges that slowed down hiring when they went international for the first time." ~~~ Let's not kid ourselves. Buyers are rarely reading our entire email the first time around. Give them a reason to open. Then give them a reason to pay attention. Leverage triggers, tension statements, and trust statements.

content: Rippling has some crazy good plays for booking meetings with enterprise HR leaders. Ken Amar runs the enterprise SDR org at Rippling. Before that, he was SDR of the Year at Outreach. Ken's whole career has been based on one bet: when everyone zigs, you zag. Here are three outbound plays his team is running right now to book quality enterprise meetings. ✅ 1. The groundswell play. Ken's reps don't open enterprise accounts by calling the CHRO. They call the HR analyst three levels down, offer a $50 gift card for 30 minutes of their time, and run a mini discovery call to learn the tech stack, the renewal date, and what's frustrating people day to day. Then they ask the analyst to multi-thread them up to their director. Connect-to-meeting rate on a normal enterprise cold call: about 5%. Multi-thread rate on the groundswell play: 50%. Five times more effective. And a $50 gift card to a Fortune 1000 CHRO is in poor taste anyway. They can afford their own lunch. ✅ 2. The Broadway ticket play. One of Ken's reps researched a CHRO in Denver. Microbiology major. Broadway fan. They sent her a leather notebook, two Broadway tickets, and a handwritten note referencing her upcoming renewal with a competitor. Two weeks later, she emailed back out of the blue. That gift generated a multimillion-dollar opportunity. There are 100 other SDRs trying to reach that same person with the same automated sequence. Be different. ✅ 3. The suite invite as the email lead. Rippling rents NBA suites in cities where they have high-value CHROs. The whole cadence is built around the invite. Only 11 seats. Open rate: 70%. Reply rate: 41%. Even the no's are warm. This lines up with the Gong data we cite all the time. Offers increase reply rate by 28%. Direct meeting asks decrease reply rate by 44%. Trade your asks for offers. ~~~ The full episode goes deeper on two more plays his team uses (ghostwritten exec emails and custom landing pages at scale), Ken's origin story at Outreach, and his hot take on why AI SDRs are just the next SEP. In this episode, we talked about: - Ken's journey from rep to Sr. Director at Rippling - Unconventional plays that book qualified meetings - The future of AI in sales Watch the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/gMZkkqkx

content: The buyer needs to RESPECT you before they can like and trust you. Damn, let that one sink in. Paul Salamanca shared that in our recent podcast episode. He has over a decade of experience as a VP of Sales. And his small but mighty BDR team at Cymon AI is crushing it right now. Two BDRs book 30-40 enterprise meetings per month between the two of them. Yes, per month. We can do many things, unintentionally, to lower our status with buyers. Behaviors that immediately signal that we're not a peer. Like saying "sorry" for cold calling. Or being overly grateful to the buyer for taking time to meet with you. Or never pushing back on their requests, or thinking about gives/gets. We can level the playing field by being true experts at what we sell, speaking to the buyer like a peer, and setting boundaries when needed. Paul and I had a killer chat about that and much more: - Paul's journey from rep to VP of Sales to founder - Pipegen techniques working to book a ton of qualified meetings - How his SDRs book 15-20+ enterprise meetings each per month - The future of AI in sales Watch the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/ghQUUKKY

content: Prospect says "will you hold the price till next month?" 3 words. "I don't know" Because the reality is, you don't. When a deal slips, you have to make a judgment call: 🅰️ Did they make a good faith effort to get it done? 🅱️ Or did you get the runaround? There are times where your champion did everything and some black swan event truly came up. In those cases, I'll close it on the 2nd, preserve goodwill so I can get expansion later, avoid the whole nonsense of re-issuing a PO. (and either way you weren't gonna ratchet it all the way back up) But if we have mutually agreed on making a good faith effort to close it by EOQ and it was NOT a mutual good faith effort. That may lead to reconsideration. ... In sales, holding equal business stature means that you get something in return when you give something up. In negotiation, you trade certainty for certainty. Or uncertainty for uncertainty. (PS: I was kinda fumbling on this until we interviewed Todd Caponi, who just dropped his negotiation course with 30MPC. It personally has helped our team a lot and I hope it helps y'all too.)
author_info: Author x Founder at 30 Minutes to President's Club | VP of Sales

content: Sales people get a bad rap because there's frankly a lot of draconian advice that tells you to manipulate your prospects. Things like: - Never share price until your customer thinks it's more - Anchor high so they feel like they got a win - Don't give price until they introduce you to the boss - Hide your product until they answer 700 interrogation questions A big part of the reason we started 30 Minutes to President's Club is because good selling doesn't feel like selling. It feels like helping. But negotiation was one of the few places where I thought that might not be true. Because the reality is: - You are often incentivized to hide price - Procurement *will* want their pound of flesh - Your next rent payment *might* be contingent on that 20% In these moments, we as salespeople are put to the test. Do I protect my wallet or protect my integrity? -- I thought all of this was true for negotiation until I met Todd Caponi. He told a story about how he put 4 levers on the table that customers could use to buy themselves a discount, then cut their own deal: 1. Volume: How much you buy 2. Timing of Cash: How fast you pay 3. Length of Commitment: How long you commit 4. Timing of Deal: How you help us predict We rolled it out to our team and it was ridiculous. When prospects realized that these were the levers they could pull and all the negotiation cards were on the table, they stopped the 17-call negotiations and just decided on what options were best for them. Shortly after, I asked Todd to launch a negotiation course with us. I'm grateful he chose to partner with us because he's one of the few people who really just wants to make the profession of sales better. And that course is available today. -- Not everyone does sales right. The moment you jump on a call, you are not only battling the natural resistance of change. But you are battling the natural resistance of eroded trust from 20 other sellers who have been taught to walk the gray lines of sales tactics. I hope this one helps sellers realize that: - You can discount less - Close deals faster - AND preserve your integrity too. Four Levers Negotiating is live today at 30MPC. I would love to hear what y'all think because I freaking love this one. (And it's on discount this week because we are willing to pay for our ability to predict launch week sales 😉)
author_info: Author x Founder at 30 Minutes to President's Club | VP of Sales

content: This is my go-to negotiation question that has saved my goose when talking to procurement. "We can consider many things. I'm curious, where's the ask coming from? 2 things happen when you ask it: 1. You figure out who's asking for the sake of asking. 2. You reveal hidden objections BEHIND a pricing objection (ie: an ankle biting competitor who has no business being in the same deal) Understand the objection before you handle it. (PS: Todd Caponi broke down how to handle literally every single pricing objection you can get on our YT channel, check it out in my bio)
author_info: Author x Founder at 30 Minutes to President's Club | VP of Sales

content: Wanna book more meetings on LinkedIn? Don't connect and pitch. Try this 2-step template: === Step 1: Blank connection request === Any pitch in the connection request will get auto deleted. If you wanna personalize it, go context only. Example: "i'd see those wrestler's ears any day" === Step 2: Use a personalization sandwich and drive back to email === "John! Not sure if you knew, but you team's full of 30MPC super fans. Not gonna do the whole connect and pitch thing. See my email by chance?" (PS: Heard you brought a magician to SKO. content: There's only one meeting in which you can lose 20% of your commission check in 30 minutes. That is the negotiation. The 5 biggest negotiation mistakes people make are: 1️⃣ Filling the silence after giving the number Instead: explain how price works up front, give the number, and then ask for their reaction. 2️⃣ Pitching when they say the price is too high Instead: Understand why they felt the price was high. They're often just saying it to say it. 3️⃣ Giving away discounts for free Any discount should give you something in return. More seats, longer term, or secured timeline. 4️⃣ Then billing terms, split payments, and waived implementation This begins death by a million cuts. You need to get the full ask and force trade-offs between price and terms. 5️⃣ Trying to lie or anchor high If you want a certain way to lose your entire check, it's because they found out you quoted them above list to pad the quote. === Instead, we've started putting all of our negotiation cards on the table up front and letting customers cut their own deal. There are 4 levers that determine our price (per the slide): 1. How much you buy 2. How fast you pay 3. How long you commit 4. How you help us forecast If a customer wants a discount, they can get it. We just tell them exactly what we would ask for in return (more seats, longer commitment, close now). This has moved anxiety in the negotiation process to 0 for us. And I didn't come up with this. Todd Caponi did. His negotiation masterclass is 100% free. Just say "negotiation machine" below and I'll send it to ya :)
author_info: Author x Founder at 30 Minutes to President's Club | VP of Sales

content: When you have no pipeline in sales: - You second guess yourself on calls where the prospect was the problem - You cling onto deals that were never going to close anyway - You avoid introducing friction into deals (ie: asking for power) - You ask your champion for permission to do everything - You discount more When you have a full pipeline: - You ask customers if they're actually going to buy this thing - You push bad deals away and let them opt back into the sales cycle - You start selling with swagger and having fun - You stop overthinking every single thing you say on a call - You know if you can't get to power you'll find a deal that will Yes, there are things you should fix in your sales process. But you will be amazed how much more clearly you will see deals for what they are when you have 20 of them. It's Monday. Go get one more.

content: We made a huge change to our pricing last month. Now we discount less, close deals faster (and do it all without the sleazy feeling of lying in a negotiation). There are 4 levers that determine our price: - Volume: How much you buy - Length of Commitment: How long you commit - Timing of Cash: How fast you pay - Timing of Deal: How you help us predict We put % amounts to each of these things and then let our customers cut their own deal. (Example: If you want a discount, commit to more volume or help us predict by agreeing on a timeline.) And it's crazy. Customers are just kinda like: "Uhm. This makes sense. Let us figure out what we want to do." So most of our negotiations end in 1-2 calls. -- It turns out traditional negotiation: - SLOWS DOWN DEALS: Because a customer will wait until the last day of the month if they know your discounts are contingent on your quota. - LOSES DEALS: Because if they find out they got quoted more than their peers, you risk the deal entirely. - SHRINKS DEALS: Because a savvy buyer picks you apart the moment they realize your pricing is fungible. - FEELS SLEAZY: Because you're punishing the champion who put their political capital on the line to get you in the budget by trying to close it at full rack. I used to do all the traditional sales tactics of anchoring high, playing chicken, etc. After I met Todd Caponi, our team does "cards face up" 4 Levers Pricing and it's truly changed the game for us. In sales it's really easy to do things the wrong way. And sometimes, "gurus" in our industry preach it and get paid for it. Todd's one of the few dudes who really just wants to make the profession of sales better. And it turns out that you can do that AND become a better seller at the same time. -- Today, you can teach your team to do the same. The 4 Levers Negotiating course is live: https://hubs.li/Q04jrPw-0 You'll learn how to be a world class negotiator while ALSO doing it the right way. I really, really hope y'all take a look bc I had so much fun in this shoot with a good friend and a good dude.

content: Live whiteboarding the org chart has completely changed the multithreading game for us in deals. Here's how to do it in 3 steps. For Context: We need to get the 3-headed hydra onboard whenever we're selling a training engagement: - The Enablement Lead to champion the program - 1-2 Sales Leaders to vouch that it'll land with reps - And then the VP/CRO (who always tips if the others are aligned) 1️⃣ Get the champion into same side selling mode. This usually happens after the discovery + demo, where they're like "ya I want this" I literally say, "this is where you and I get to conquer the world together. Will u be my friend 🥹" This makes them feel like they're part of the process. 2️⃣ Draw the "golden path" to get a deal done. Map out the key stakeholders that you need to tip the sale. For us, we literally find the 3-headed trifecta and screenshot their names / faces / titles. This lets you teach them how to buy and avoid random multithreading to irrelevant or below-the-line stakeholders. 3️⃣ Ask them to help you understand the shadow org chart You see the external org chart. But there's a POLITICAL org chart that they know. This is where we get to ask: "Who's the sales leader who has your VP's ear?" We know if we tip THAT person, the VP/CRO will tip too. -- No one likes to hear "who's going to sign off on this?" as if you're gonna steamroll over them to get in front of their boss. Turn your champion into a 2nd sales rep on your team. That is same side selling 101.

content: Sellers: It’s a 4-day work week. If you haven’t made your cold calls yet, don’t let this be a 0 pipe gen week. Instead: 1. Avoid the prospecting crash diet A short week goes to hell when you try to stuff a full week of prospecting into 4 days. Most reps fall behind on day 1 then say f*ck it and completely drop the ball on prospecting because there’s no shot to hit the goal. Instead… 2. Set a REASONABLE prospecting commit If you normally commit to 200, commit to 125 (instead of 160 for example). Set a commit you can ACTUALLY hit. Top reps have a 100% say-to-do ratio. 3. Block it on your calendar NOW Do not leave it for when you have “spare time” 4. Cancel ALL unnecessary meetings Random 1:1s, optional lunch and learns, HR nonsense. Push it all to next week. 5. Push other meetings to the AFTERNOON Protect the hours between 9-12 so you do the ugly thing you don’t want to do first thing in the morning. Pick up the phone. This is your chance to get ahead when everyone else falls behind 👊🏽

content: Most managers (and parents) think they're disciplining their team so they can help them improve. Apparently, they're actually making them dumber. I had Bryan Post on Make It Happen Mondays this week. Bryan has spent 30 years working with highly stressed families and children. He's written 22 books. And he said something that completely reframed how I think about leadership. "The definition of discipline is to teach, not to punish." he shared that when you put someone under stress, their thinking becomes confused, distorted, and their short-term memory shuts off. So when a manager "disciplines" a rep by putting them on blast in a team meeting or managing through fear, they're not teaching anything. They're suppressing that person's ability to learn and retain information. Think about how that applies to sales too. When a buyer is stressed, overwhelmed, scared to make a decision, piling on more pressure doesn't move them forward. It shuts them down. Bryan actually challenged the entire Challenger Sale model on this point. If a buyer is already afraid, a "challenging" them with FUD is adding fear on top of fear which creates a compounding negative effect. Instead, he suggests to slow down and get curious. Ask "what is it about this decision that's stressing you so much?" That one question changes the whole dynamic because now you're in connection, not confrontation. This conversation went deep into stress, cortisol, fight or flight, the science of fear, and how it all shows up in how we lead, parent, and sell. It's a little different than my normal interview but I this one was super interesting for me and should be for you too. When you need to correct someone on your team or help a buyer through a tough decision, are you teaching or punishing?

content: Is curiosity something you're born with, or can you teach it? I ask almost every podcast guest this question. Based on their feedback and my observations, my opinion is that genuine curiosity is something you're born with BUT you can develop it by getting someone interested in something first. Interest is the on-ramp to curiosity. But, this is only my opinion. So, I got curious and wanted to see if there was any data/science that backed this up and used AI feed my curiosity. Here's what it came up with: (AI content) George Loewenstein at Carnegie Mellon published the "information gap" theory. Curiosity only fires when you notice a gap between what you know and what you want to know. No gap, no curiosity. You need to know enough about something to realize what you don't know. That's why interest has to come first. A UC Davis neuroscience study found that curious people remember completely unrelated information better. Curiosity improves your memory about everything around you, not one thing. (end of AI content) Think about what that means on a sales call. Reps who are genuinely curious about helping the client don't just pick up on the client's direct answers, they pick up on side comments, body language and throwaway lines that nobody else catches. Here's one way I've applied this for 25 years. When reps work with me, we dive into the nuanced details of their ICP so they can tier out their accounts and build a list of top 25 Tier1 accounts to start prospecting into. After they build the list but before I allow them to start prospecting into them I have them do one more exercise. I want them to go look to find a reason they personally want to work with them, and not just because they fit the ICP. Is it their leadership? Do their values align? Is it a really cool product/service they could see selling themselves? I want them to find a personal connection to the account because one you make that connection, you become more curious which leads to more research which leads to better and more authentic messaging which leads to better and more fulfilling results. You don't build or train curiosity by telling reps to "be more curious." You create the conditions for it and AI can either be an accelerator or replacement for it. We went from search engines to answer engines. Search made us dig a little, which created gaps, which created curiosity. Answer engines skip the process entirely. For people who aren't curious, that's dangerous. For curious people, AI is the greatest research and curiosity tool ever built. I went from a question I've been asking for years to published neuroscience in minutes. I also didn't take the first answer it gave me. I pushed back and went deeper each time. The AI didn't make me curious. My interest in the topic made me curious which made the AI incredibly useful. Stop looking for the answers, get interested and be curious... #MakeItHappen

content: "Life imitates Art far more than Art imitates Life" - Oscar Wilde, The Decay of Lying. I'm starting to believe that Art predicts or even dictates life at this point. Have you ever seen the movie Idiocracy? If not, set your expectations low if you decide to watch it. It's not exactly an Oscar winner but unfortunately seems to be where we're heading these days. Long story short, it's about an idiot in today's world (Luke Wilson) who gets frozen and forgotten about for 500 years. When he wakes up, the US population has gotten progressively dumber and the "smartest" person is the president. Even though he was a moron by today's standards, Luke Wilson is now a genius compared to everyone else in the future. One of my favorite aspects of the movie is the story about one of their wardrobe decisions. In the movie, the costume department had a shoestring budget and needed cheap, futuristic-looking footwear. They found a pair of the foam clogs from a unknown boating startup at the time. The producers initially expressed concern, asking what would happen if the shoes became mainstream by the time the film was released. The costume designer reportedly laughed it off, assuring him that no one would ever wear such horrible-looking things. Because 20th Century Fox shelved the film for two years, Idiocracy did not hit theaters until September 2006. In that exact window, those "horrible looking things," now known as Crocs, exploded from an obscure boating startup into a massive global fashion phenomenon. If you watch the movie, every single person in the future is wearing Crocs. So, what was originally intended to be a cheap, futuristic, and intentionally stupid-looking gag ended up accidentally predicting the future of footwear. You're welcome :)

content: Personalization at scale is an oxymoron. When everyone is using AI to personalize, the noise goes up., not the bar. I'm seeing it everywhere right now. Inboxes are flooded with AI-generated emails that all sound the same. "I noticed your company recently..." followed by a paragraph that could've been written to anyone. content: What does mentorship mean to you? I was always a bit uneasy having "A" mentor. I looked/look for different people who had specific traits that I admired and wanted to learn from. Mentorship doesn't have to be a formal thing and you don't have to be in a leadership role to be a mentor. As a matter of fact, most of the people that I would consider mentors don't even know they are one to me. If there is one that I would highlight, it's my now good friend, Jeff Hoffman. When I was 23 years old leading sales for my first start up and kicking the sh-t out of all our competition, I thought I was the best sales rep out there. The I went to a public sales training workshop that Jeff was presenting at and realized I wasn't close. Without him knowing it, he inspired me to be better. Later on I ended up working for him at Basho and continuing to learn from him. I don't think he knew it at the time but I was paying attention to absolutely everything he did. Now, years later, I still consider him a mentor but now much more of a friend. I share this story because I was reminded of it after having a fantastic conversation with Colleen Stanley, CEO on this week's episode of the Make it Happen Monday podcast. Colleen is an expert in emotional intelligence and mentorship in sales and sales leadership. We talked how mentorship is more important now than ever and how companies don't necessarily need to have a formal mentor program but they need to build a culture of mentorship. We also talked about EQ and how to train and develop it which I think is more important than ever now that IQ is a commodity. Take a listen to the clip below and listen to the full podcast wherever you get yours. What does mentorship mean to you and who do you consider a mentor?

content: For 25 years I've taught prospecting. The last few weeks I've been doing it myself, at scale, inside Claude. I built two outbound motions running entirely on Apollo.io's MCP connector, which puts the whole stack in one place. The data and execution meet me inside the AI I already use every day, and the friction of tab-switching is gone. Tier 1 targets ex-Salesforce VPs of Sales, people I have warm context with from training their old teams. Tier 2 is brand new. It's manufacturing-specific, targeting VPs and Directors of Sales at industrial accounts where I have zero existing relationships. Pure cold outbound. The actual numbers, real and current: - TIER 1: 14.3% reply rate across 35 delivered contacts, with one demo booked in the first wave (industry cold-to-meeting average is 0.5-1.5%, mine is closer to 3%) - TIER 2 (eight days in): 20% open rate, 18% click rate, 54 contacts already worked through the sequence. Every morning the routine is the same. I open Claude, describe the next prospects I want to work, and Apollo's MCP pulls their data, verified emails, and recent triggers, then pushes them straight into the right sequence. The whole motion takes me about 20 minutes a day. I am not bringing up Apollo because they're a sponsor. I'm bringing up Apollo because the sequence that built these numbers runs through their MCP into Claude. The tool meets me where I already work instead of asking me to come to it. This is what AI as leverage, not a crutch, actually looks like. The framework still matters more than the tool, but when the tool shows up inside the AI you already use, the friction disappears and the numbers start to compound. Practicing the craft beats teaching the craft. Doing both is even better. #MakeItHappen

content: The easiest way to get promoted isn't by doing good work and hoping you're seen. It's by having this conversation with your boss, their skip-level, and a peer or two: "My hope is to be considered for xyz role when another one opens, and I'd be grateful for your feedback. Do you think I'm ready for the role now, or are their skill set gaps I can be working on over the next year to ensure I have the best shot at it? What can I do today to make sure I'm as strong of a candidate as I can be?" Speak early and loudly about your goals, be open to what you have to work on, and ensure your sponsors and mentors are looped in, too, so they can keep their ears out and advocate for you as well. Bonus: you may not like or even trust the feedback you get from your leader, which is why those mentors and sponsors are key. They can either give you more grounded advice, or reinforce what you received, even if you didn't like it. #samsales

content: Chuck: Sam, you're amazing at demand generation! Sam: mmm...mmmhmm... (Googles: what...is...demand...generation...) In the early days of my career, I heard terms like the above that made no sense to me, as well as: EBIDTA Run rate Ratable ACV, NRR, TCV, MRR, you down with OPP? wait...I got distracted Infosec Cancellation for convenience SPIF Tranche deal LTV:CAC SQL, MQL, SAL, You get it. If you find yourself in the same spot, and with a ton of that language being specific to how you sell, check out how this small Grammarly tip you can use: Take the email you have drafted, drop it into Grammarly, and just ask: "Does this make sense for the buyer persona or what can I add/recraft?" "I wrote this but want it to resonate with the CMO of this org - can you refine it for me?" "This feels like it was written at a 3rd grade level, can you level up the executive presence of it but without words that are casually used in business?" What I love about the outcome is that I learn my new terminology, I make sure the content is right for my buyers, and Grammarly refines the text to help me think of how to level up one of my favorite things: writing. #samsales #GrammarlyPartner

content: I met my husband's friends for the first time in 2016 at a party. A BOLD choice for a third date, but I digress... We left and Ryan said, "They LOVED you!" As peachy as I occasionally am, I said, "I know why - it's because I spent the night getting to know them and asking questions about their lives." It's a super power of mine and while occasionally alarming to some, my love of asking questions has served me in my personal life, but so much so in sales. NPR did a great bit on the likeability of people who ask questions, but... ...how they are exponentially more likeable if they ask active listening questions. And, Forrester found that the number one thing B2B buyers want from their sellers is: Active listeners. Someone who asks a question, truly listens, and asks another based on the answer they heard (read: not one that is self-serving or qualifying). So, if we know that asking active listening questions makes us more likeable, and that people buy from people they like, more often than not, it's a home run for all our sellers. But the question is: where does it stack up on our training priorities? And have you considered the generational changes in how a 23 yr old communicates vs. a 53 yr old? It may come naturally to one and feel invasive to another. Seller, BDR, accountant, wealth manager, CSM...we can level up every person that engages with our buyers with just one tweak, and by teaching the art of great conversation. #samsales

content: Why does a post with 50 likes get 10,000 impressions but one with 300 gets 5,000, and what's a critical practice we run for our LI Exec Branding clients with the former? The Math: Take your impressions and divide them by your likes. The output = your Dark Social Score. Baseline benchmark = 40 Our standard aim = 50 Dark Social optimal traction = 70 (and any nominal increase over that) 10,000 impressions / 50 likes = 200 DSS 🔥 5,000 impressions / 300 likes = 16 DSS 😢 So, why this disparity? It's in the type of post you share. The former tends to be pieces of content that shared something tactical, inspiring, actionable. The latter tends to be celebratory. The former gets lifted off of LI and shared in texts, Slack, etc. People show up (impression), read, look at your profile, and leave, all without engaging (hence the disparity). The latter is your network celebrating you which is why your engagement is high and impressions are low. ******************************* Why Dark Social Matters: You show up here for a reason: brand awareness. Most clients come to us to solve a few challenges: - Their company or CXOs are relatively unknown - Everyone knows their organization exists, but they're a large, faceless brand - They're working to be the thought leaders of their categories - the ones turned to each time by journalists and conference organizers. They show up to teach and for reach, and when that DSS sky rockets, it tells you that this is what your market is interested in, which is a data point to capitalize on. ******************************** The CRITICAL step to capitalizing on your posts and DSS: Operationalizing the GTM motions of your executives' brands. Here's one of a dozen plays we run: systemize the review of your profile views each week, and connect with bespoke messaging to the 2nd and 3rd degree connections that are in your target ICP. Not only will you systematically grow your network but you'll remove the gate that stood between them seeing your content the first time, you'll up the chances of them seeing the content again, and you'll create a drip campaign of content that they'll one day engage with, and will be seen by their network, full of people who look just like them. #samsales #LIEB

content: Self-deprecating humor is something we know, but what about the self-deprecating postscript that builds client relationships and might even help you get promoted faster? Here's what I mean. When I make a mistake, you'll usually find me adding one extra layer that says, "I know better." Sam: Can we meet at 1pm or 2pmET? Buyer: Sure, what day though? Sam: Ah, the date would be helpful, wouldn't it... :) Does Tuesday work?* Boss: Can you shoot me the deck for tomorrow? Sam: You bet, attached! Boss: It has someone else's logo on it...? Sam: Ah, thanks for the catch! Let me sweep the slides and make sure it's buttoned up - that would have been impossibly embarrassing.* The normal answers I see are: "Would Tuesday work?" without acknowledgement of the missed detail. It's small, but communicates volumes when not acknowledged. and "Fixed!" without acknowledgement that sending a deck with typos is sloppy or without proactively communicating the extra attention to detail. When we skip that postscript, we communicate the wrong thing about ourselves, which is that we didn't know better, don't respect the person's time, or don't see the problem, none of which is something we want our leader or buyers to think. Our buyers' trust will erode quickly. Our leaders will feel the need to address basic professionalism in our 1:1 Instead, add the extra line, build humor in when you can, and triple check the work in the future. We all make 100 mistakes a day - it's what we do and how we proactively communicate afterwards that makes us different. #samsales

content: Someone referred us a lead this week, an intro over email to a buyer who could use our services. Here's the exact playbook I use to level up what everyone on that email expects: 1. Urgency - while I can't always do it, I try to reply to the buyer before they reply to me. 2. Two seconds of Show Me You Know Me first - who are they, who do we know, have they worked for clients*, where do they live, what have they posted about, how can I connect with them better in my reply. *If they know other people who have hired us, I'm prioritizing that for additional creditability 3. My reply structure: Thanks to the introductions Some SMYKM notes/connections Happy to find time - send me some dates/time (no calendar link, court them) Thanks again to the referral, say I'm moving referrer to bcc* *Sometimes reps only reply to the buyer, which leaves your referrer in the dark (and annoyed). I also like to thank them twice in the email vs. just at the top + "moving you to BCC" which feels a little rushed/greedy, IMO. 4. BCC myself on the email - not everyone replies and sometimes we need a follow up nudge. I don't want the opp to fall through the cracks, and this isn't an email I send as part of a cadence. 4. Another thank you email to the referral source under separate cover. "Really appreciate you thinking of us and sticking your neck out for us..." 5. An update to the referrer as things progress. This can be once mid-way if it's a larger ENT deal that will take time, or only at the end when you win/lose, if a short sales cycle. Closing the loop speaks volumes here. 6. If we win the deal, we're sending a handwritten note to the referrer or a gift that...you guessed it...Show Me You Know Me's them. It doesn't need to cost much - a book goes a long way - but it's one extra touch of gratitude for revenue you wouldn't have had without them. All of these steps are important for not only mapping you out as a completely different kind of seller, but of proving to the referrer that they can continue to gamble their reputation on you with more referrals. #samsales

content: One of my favorite workshops we run is all around ghosting in the sales cycle. Here are two examples of what we recently uncovered and how you can run your own. How To: Limit the sessions to eight reps Each one brings a deal that's stalled They bring the emails they sent, the history, the call notes We dissect it all and tell you why they likely ghosted, how to course-correct for next time, and what to do now to at least get a reply. Bonus: run this for your team, but invite two peer leaders to do the dissecting. You'll be amazed what a different perspective can bring. Two recent scenarios: 1. A client sent a Show Me You Know Me email. Two weeks later, they followed up to ask for time. The response: No, I'm on PTO these next two weeks. The takeaways: - Email two should never come that late. <48 hours after email one. - I asked if they'd noticed the time stamp of the reply. It was about an hour after the second email went out. - The rep's leader was recently at an event with the buyer's leader Imagine if we'd caught this sooner. Imagine if email two had gone out 44 hours later, if the buyer had replied in the same time window. We'd potentially have gotten the meeting before PTO, started the process, and have gotten details the leaders could have discussed at the event rather than still working to book time in. 2. A buyer showed interest in a platform. Rep replies, feature-dumps, silence in return. The "just following up" emails ensue, and now we're stuck. The easiest way to get unstuck here is to simply own the mistake with a script like this: Mark, I fear I didn't do justice to how our platform can help, mostly because I wrote you an email telling you what we do vs. what we solve. For your specific use case, here are three challenges I can help you with... If the window hasn't closed to win your business, I know I can make great use of your time, and would so appreciate the consideration. The reply? "Your self-awareness won me over, and I was candidly stuck because your competition wrote me almost the same first email you did. Happy to make time to talk." Small incremental catches and changes can impact deals, but the best part is that coaching to this nitty gritty stuff will them impact every other motion that rep, and those listening, will take. #samsales

content: I credit a lot of my success in sales to something we teach every day: good old fashioned manners. I send thank you notes when we win deals and I send them when I lose them. I follow up with people who referred me deals, and close the loop. I send thank you gifts to anyone that helped me win, big or small, deals. Gin, books, champagne, $500 to dinner in Chicago, cupcakes, flowers, you name it. I follow through and deliver something, even if it's not what I promised, by when I said I'd do it by. I can count the times I've missed this on my hands. I put my niceties at the bottom of my emails, to be as authentic as I can. I show empathy when someone is going through something, even if it costs us progress or a deal. When someone goes out of their way to bring value to us, I proactively say thank you and close the loop on the status. I proactively look for chances to connect others or drive value for them, when it has no impact on us. I try to reply to every client email within 24 hours, even if it's a "received, will hit you back by X date". When we describe #samsales, we always say that everything we teach has a manners-drive, buyer-centric approach, and I love the eyebrow raise it always seems to get. In a me-me-me world, I think you'll find that your buyers will take notice when your sellers inject a little Emily Post into their process. The status quo is the me-me-me - imagine how we'll stand out when we're exactly the opposite of what they expect. #samsales

content: "LinkedIn recommends that we do more video, so we do, and they flop. Why would they recommend something that doesn't seem to work?" That...is a great question. Here's the thing, video can be effective in three ways: 1. You're a notable exec celeb and people want to hear whatever you share (Jon Gray @ Blackstone) 2. You're not one but built a following and people love hearing you (influencers in general) 3. None of the above, but it humanizes you + warms buyers up to you What the recommendation misses is an understanding of the creators and the audience on LinkedIn specifically. We might be in a TikTok + reels era, but those making content here, especially at an executive level, aren't adept with that style, nor does it fit their executive persona. So what might stop the scroll on another platform, doesn't here, because it lacks the scroll-stopping words or style. Further? A video requires the viewer to learn at the pace of the video, not at theirs or with the ability to skim, learn on their terms; so, they move along. Further? 90%+ of video is watched on mute, so if you're not captioning your content, you're losing a lot of your dwell time. Further? You may create content without any media training, which can actually damage your brand vs. help it. Video CAN work well for specific use cases, and you should have some in your featured section to let buyers/recruiters easily find it, but a stop-the-scroll text hook + an image or infographic will work over video, for the great majority of content creators on this platform. #samsales

content: Should your subject line on an outbound sales email be personalized, relevant to their challenge, or both? Personalized only. That subject line is a work horse and nothing that you say in the email will matter if your subject line doesn't crush its one job: to pique your reader's curiosity enough that it earns you an open. When we personalize it like: SUB: Five years + Your London Retreat + Maille Cornichons, we show the buyer we made an authentic effort. When we personalize it + make it relevant like: SUB: Five years + scaling your team's AI usage with Claude, we suggest that this is a sales email, and we burn the chance at the open. Can't find anything to Show Me You Know Me your buyers? Then we go to relevance, but you still make it personalized. SUB: (CEO's Name)'s recent note about scaling your team's AI usage with Claude ^^pull content from their site, press releases, articles, etc. that suggests they need you, and then tie that into your subject line in some way. #samsales #SMYKM

content: In 2019, I left LinkedIn and was asked to interview for a VP role out in the Bay Area. "We've been trying to fill this role for seven months, but haven't found the right candidate." I flew out to SF, finalized my interviews, and got the offer a day or two later. The company was 1000+ employees, had just finalized their last Series @ 100M+, and made me an offer that was 15K lower than my last salary as an AE. I politely declined and offered the note that base salary wasn't in line with market rates, and I suspected they'd have a challenge finding the talent they were looking for. They politely said they understood and we moved on. Several weeks later, they came back. "We'd like to revisit the offer...", which came back at 2.3x the initial base salary. ****************************************************************** It's a tough market right now, but I think that actually gives anyone hiring an edge. While some companies might lower their offers and see this as a chance to save, I think this serves as a competitive advantage. If you've been seeking talent for seven months, and you find the one you want, don't lowball them and do court them as you would a client. - Fly them first class to interview - Put them up at a great hotel + have a note/flowers/swag waiting for them when they check in (as Michael Nelson did when I interviewed for an AE role!) - Send a car for them the morning of - Give them more than they ask for, even if it's nominal, it says something And, if you're the interviewee, and don't know how to handle the, "What base salary are you seeking?", swipe my script: "It's a hard question the answer as I'd need to know all the components - OTE, equity, signing bonus, 401K match, etc.; I'd love to take a look at the full picture when you've got it!" It's counter to what traditional negotiation will tell you, but it's an honest answer, and also suggests what components make up the other offers you're seeing. To the honest point, I'd take $0 base salary, if I was working for, say, Apple, and knowing I was getting issued 10,000 full vested stock grants annually. ;) #samsales

content: I talked to a VP at one of the largest sales tech companies in the world last week. She said something that made me do The Rock eyebrow raise. The B2B teams deploying AI the fastest are about to discover their biggest problem isn't technology. It's their people. Me: I was like oooh tell me more. Angela Garinger Bennett said once we strip away all the admin work from sellers... once the agents are running and the workflows are humming... you're going to see a massive talent gap where people are going to want to skill up their team with this free time. Hearing that from someone inside 100s of these organizations made it way more real. Because the conversations I'm having with leaders right now are completely different than 12 months ago. When I work with organizations on outbound.. it's about how to send LinkedIn messages.. cold call.. cold email etc. Now it's... Morgan our reps have all the tools... They have the AI workflows.. But they can't: ↳ Hold a real conversation with a buyer. ↳ Think creatively on their feet. ↳ Connect the dots between what they're seeing and what the prospect actually needs. Slowly but surely people are starting to outsource their entire brain to AI and now they can't sell to a human as a human. Uh oh.. that is not good. And the part that really got me... she brought up newer sellers coming into the workforce will adopt AI fast yet have no idea how to even hold a conversation. Which will lead to them not wanting to sell because they would rather be behind the AI.. So.. I was in deep thought like what is next then? Here's where I've landed. AI will handle the non-soulful work. Research.. list building.. etc. But there's a deeper layer none of that touches. How do you show up when the admin is gone and it's just you and the buyer? How do you become someone people actually want to keep talking to? How do you think critically instead of waiting for AI to hand you the answer? The work I've committed to is simple.. develop better humans who happen to sell. The skills follow from there. Because in 6-8 months every team will have the same AI agents. The only thing separating your team from every competitor is how your people show up. Most teams aren't ready for that conversation yet. But it's coming.
author_info: Coaching B2B sales teams to sound human in their outbound when everyone else sounds like AI | CEO @ AMP Social | SKO Speaker

content: I spent 9 weeks straight in Arizona without traveling which I have not done since 2021. Here's 20 things it taught me about life that might change your pace. 1. I got closer to God's presence. 2. UK rap on a night drive through the desert. 3. The gym at 2pm when nobody's there is therapy. 4. I love playing pickleball... see you at the Olympics 5. Quicktime drinks after a long day in Arizona is clutch. 6. Spending quality time at church meeting new people. 7. My morning declarations change the way I see myself. 8. Morning walks before I look at my phone kept me calm. 9. Reading at Barnes and Noble for 2 hours is underrated. 10. Re-playing Red Dead Redemption gives me so much joy. 11. Applying Japanese principles regulates your nervous system. 12. Saying no to things that drain me got easier every single week. 13. I am genuinely in love with creating YouTube videos right now. 14. Embracing new outbound tactics that I never thought of before. 15. Working with clients got significantly better when I slowed down. 16. Cigar nights during the week paired with deep convos hit different 17. Random 2-hour conversations with my close friends were life giving. 18. Being present allows you to see things you've been missing for years. 19. I don't need to be on LinkedIn 5 hours a day to build a great business 20. My Head of Operations Stefano Canducci i is a gem of a human who I love working with as I had more time to be present The biggest thing I learned though... In this world we can create any environment we want to at any time. So.. I decided to spend LESS time on social media and MORE time with friends locally. This one commitment alone to evolved me as a person. • My Sales Team Six program got better. • It was more impactful for my clients. • My energy shifted. I didn't need to do more. I needed to be more. And I can 100% do it. That was the coolest part to find out. P.S. What's one thing you learned in this season that changed how you move?

content: I used to base my forecast on feelings. When I implemented the Jordan Rules I increased my close rate by 20%. Here is how I did it. For years, my forecasting was vibes-based. Because you know me I love the vibes. "I think this one's close." "They seemed interested in the call." "Should close by the end of month... maybe." Then I'd close no deals because they were stalled and wonder what happened. My effort was not in question, it was how I was tracking the deal. I was making decisions based on what buyers SAID, not what they DID. Here’s how I used my Jordan rules with Consensus to take this to the next level: I started tracking buyer behavior, not just buyer words. ↳ Did they actually watch the demo? Or just say they would? ↳ Which sections did they replay? ↳ Did they share it with anyone else? ↳ How long did the CFO spend on pricing? This is the data that actually predicts outcomes. So for my Jordan rules, I know that if a deal goes over 45 days then they are not as interested, if they don’t view the summary email more than 2 times that means it is not being forwarded around and if they do not watch my demo video breaking down my offer that means no one is watching it and interest is low. Now my forecast calls sound different: (my forecast calls are me, myself and I so bare with me here) 𝗕𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲: "I feel good about Acme Corp." 𝗔𝗳𝘁𝗲𝗿: "Acme Corp has 6 stakeholders who've watched the demo. Average watch time is 12 minutes. The VP of Ops replayed the integration section 3 times. This deal is legit." That's not a vibe feeling. That's evidence. Consensus recently acquired Saleo and Peel, which means the platform now supports the entire product journey, from self-guided exploration to live demos to buyer intelligence. So your champion isn't just sharing a demo anymore. They're sharing an experience that can answer questions, adapt to what buyers care about, and give your team visibility into who's engaged, what's resonating, and where the deal stands. Stop forecasting with vibes. Start forecasting with real data. #ConsensusPartner

content: I am seeing multiple companies that cut their SDR teams in January to "save money" now put up multiple SDR job openings to build back out their team. This is a cycle I believe we will continue to see. As I have been saying for months as leaders we should be hyper focused on developing our talent then worry about AI every single day. I am pro AI don't get me wrong. Claude power user over here my friends. Yet I am also pro human. And I keep asking this question to leaders: "What happens when your team gets all this free time. What is next? I get mostly crickets here. Which I understand why... the main job to be done right now is build a foundation of AI to compliment outbound efforts. As AI compliments the efforts though.. that leads to the SDR role evolving. It's where your future leaders learn to handle rejection, read people, think on their feet, and create something from nothing. It's why I am still bullish on developing the skills of the SDR team because it will elevate your entire organization if these reps learn how to really be sellers. Because copying and pasting a prompt into an AI over and over again.. is not going to develop the skills. I mean a bot could do that.... A bot can send a thousand emails.... • It can't read the room on a cold call. • It can't connect a prospect's LinkedIn post to the problem they mentioned in passing. • It can't build the relationship that turns a maybe into a yes. And it definitely can't get promoted to AE and carry your pipeline in 18 months. The companies that develop their SDRs as humans... build the strongest revenue orgs. If it's treated as disposable... you end up rebuilding the same team every 2 years and are left scratching their heads. I'm spending 80% of my time right now running my Sales Team Six program for SDR/AE on this exact thing. How to develop business acumen... connecting the dots.. and embracing the craft of doing outbound in a way no AI can replicate. SDRs are the farm system for your entire revenue organization. Don't be the company running for the oxygen mask in 2027 because you cut off the pipeline in 2026.

content: 2018: 2 million impressions per month on LinkedIn. 2026: 70% less reach. And I've never been more at peace with it. I know.. sounds crazy. Let me explain. I've been on this platform for 10 years. Over 6,000 posts..... which is wild to even type out. And I was consumed by it. Like a little obsessive to be fair... • I would be extremely upset when a post didn't perform. • Disappointed in myself. • Staying up at night trying to reverse engineer why things didn't work. LinkedIn was my identity. If a post went well I was flying.. if it flopped I would spiral. So two years ago I decided I couldn't live like that anymore. So I started putting in the work to untie my identity from this platform and tie it to something deeper. My relationships. My craft. My faith. Here's what happened... My reach dropped by 70%. And yeah I could always improve on that. But something else happened.. my client work inside Sales Team Six got significantly better. My relationships in Arizona deepened. I reconnected with God in a way I never had before. And I was like wow.. my joy is back. Less impressions. More impact. And here's what I noticed with the sales leaders I work with too. If you obsess too much on the metrics.. it can lead to mad stress. The leaders who shifted from volume to depth of conversation saw their pipeline actually grow. So as you experience the algo shift that's frustrating everyone right now.. I get it. I've been the person screaming into the void about reach. But what if this is the best thing that could've happened to your business...? What if reaching fewer people is freeing you up to go deeper with the right ones? What if the platform getting noisier is the push you needed to pick up the phone, show up in person, and build relationships that don't depend on an algorithm? You can do both. Create content AND go deep with relationships. But if you had to pick one to obsess over.. pick the relationships. Maybe this is the season to stop performing and start becoming. The algorithm will change. Relationships won't.

content: My take is that the way we're chasing AI efficiency is about to set the B2B sales profession back 10 years. Before the pitchforks come out.... hear me out. I'm deep in AI... I love me some Claude and I use it every day. I understand the goal of AI is to make your 20-person team operate like 40. That's the multiplier and I'm all for it. And... I know your board is asking about AI, which creates executive urgency to adopt AI at a faster rate. Where my take is coming from though is I went to a conference last week and every conversation was the same... “AI is going to free up our reps’ time.” Ok yes we agree....cool... I kept asking this follow up question. Free up time to do what exactly? “More selling.” “More calling.” Ok but that's like one hour.. then what? What's the actual game plan when reps have more free time? Crickets. Because we all know the way things are moving the AI transformation will happen fast.. Two quarters from now the AI layer will be table stakes. Every team will have it and every competitor will be running the same playbooks. Which means the only thing that separates your team from theirs is how your people show up. The last thing we want on our sales team is them losing critical thinking skills and never learning business acumen which would upset a future buyer. As sales leaders we need to imagine beyond just selling: → Tailored campaigns with direct mails and multi-threading → Spending time learning the industry with podcasts and increasing business acumen → Live engagement in communities where your buyers actually spend time → Getting in the field... events, dinners, faces across tables That's the game after the AI dust settles guiding the reps to be more magnetic, intuitive and consistent humans and I'm already obsessing over it. For example, I've been locked in on dropping body fat for the last 8 months. That commitment is specific. So the activity is specific... walking 10k steps daily, pickleball, lots of protein, working out 3x a week. The commitment defined the activity. Your AI strategy needs the same thing. Don't ONLY focus on freeing up time. Define what the freed time is FOR before you deploy the tools. Bookmark this... Come back in 6-8 months... I promise you the conversation on every sales floor will be: “How do I get my reps to think again and not talk like a robot?” The leaders who built the human muscle DURING the AI transformation won't have that problem. The question was never “how do we free up time.” The question is “what are we freeing it for?” If we can all answer that, we'll have a better sales community. P.S. Sales leaders... what's your team actually doing with the time AI is freeing up? I've been asking this at every event and nobody has a clear answer yet. Prove me wrong in the comments.

content: This one Claude prompt replaced 3 hours of buyer research for every team I work with down to 5 minutes. I timed it.. so no lies here. So most of the teams that you coach and lead find a signal that says... "I see your company just raised funding"... type stuff that every other rep is also saying. So I built a prompt that does the deep research in about 5 minutes. And I mean actually deep... not some fluff insight. I'm talking specific challenges tied to the role.... What buying signals happen within your inbound process.. Trigger events that signal buying readiness... Even the reasons they might resist your solution so your reps aren't blindsided on calls. Here's the prompt: "You are a B2B intelligence expert with 20+ years of experience researching buyer personas and creating targeted messaging strategies. Conduct a deep dive on the following persona: [insert Persona] in [insert Industry]. Identify the top 3 pain points most relevant to this persona's role and business context in (current year). Look at my inbound conversations and find 3 correlation points. List 3 trigger events that typically create readiness to buy. Outline 3 reasons this persona might resist your solution. Ensure all output is practical, specific, and grounded in real-world B2B dynamics. Now explain this to me so I know you understand the process." So.. I run this inside Claude as a project. That way the context stays loaded and every rep on the team can access it without re-entering the setup every time. But here's the part most people miss and it drives me crazy... Don't have your teams just copy and paste this.... Because AI doesn't know your buyer.... AI knows patterns about your buyer. ...The rep's job is to take that intelligence and make it HUMAN... to connect a dot the AI couldn't see. That's the workflow we build inside our program Sales Team Six. The teams using this workflow are starting more conversations because their messages sound like someone who actually studied the buyer. Not someone who prompted a bot and hit send. P.S. Anyone else have any cool AI workflow stories to share? P.S.S for longer form videos.. going to put my YT channel in comments.

content: After a decade in outbound I think I finally figured out what I actually believe. When I started my business in 2023 I thought the answer was just more content... Level up the content and everything follows.... Then AI hit and I got nervous. If I'm being honest I started wondering if SDRs were going to be completely eradicated. But then I watched what happened as people experienced AI. It's done an amazing job helping reps research in minutes, spot the right buyers faster, and move deals along. At the same time... it created numbness in the marketplace because everyone started sounding the same. And that's when it clicked. I kept telling sales teams "be more human." But that's too broad to actually teach. You could be a human and be dull to be fair. So here's what I actually mean: Magnetic: buyers want to keep talking to your reps because they feel the intent behind every message. Intuitive: when something goes off script they don't freeze. They read the room and connect the dots. Consistent: same energy... Same system.... Every single day.... Not just when quota is due. I call this the MIC method. And it's what I've built my entire business around at AMP Social. This isn't theory...My friend Jordan Lucas operates exactly like this and it's why he breaks into enterprise accounts. Sierra Arnold schedules 17 meetings a month off social selling alone. Trayco Ross sent 288 messages and booked 19 meetings with a 20% reply rate. That's what I was like wait a minute... content is amazing. AI is great. Yet the real unique advantage is doing all of that and having amazing SDRs who can stand out in a magnetic way. So no the function will still live on. Within 8-12 months every sales team will have AI agents finding the same insights with the click of a button.... The research is about to be commoditized. The only thing that will separate your team from every competitor is how your people show up. I don't pretend to know your business better than you.... But I know how to make your SDRs/AEs magnetic, intuitive, and consistent. That I can do. That's the mission at AMP Social. That's what Sales Team Six builds in our 4 month program. The AI will get better. But will your team? P.S. Question to ask yourself this morning.. are your reps magnetic, intuitive, and consistent...? or just busy to be busy
