content: I believe SDR/AE teams need to spend less time learning AI and way more time on business acumen. Yeah.. I said it. Again, I am pro AI yet you still need the skills to use it right. I spent the past few days talking to sales leaders at an event and the same question kept coming up.. "Ok I get AI is here.. but what should I actually have my reps doing?" And then I had a That's So Raven vision face. Leaders are telling me that they are watching SDRs/AEs copy and paste their AI research into a doc without understanding the context of what they even copied and pasted. Like.. have we lost the plot here? Look at this point AI is table stakes. We all get that it's here and we need to use it. Alright awesome. BUT if your team only knows how to use AI to copy and paste research WITHOUT connecting the dots to the actual business outcomes... you have a real problem. Within 8-12 months every sales team will have agents that find the same insights with the click of a button. Research is about to be commoditized. That leaves critical and creative thinking as a high value skill. So leaders think about your reps when they reach out to an account... 1. Do they understand how that business makes money? 2. Do they know why THIS specific person would want to respond? 3. Can they explain the AI output without reading a script? If the answer is no... all the AI research in the world has become worthless. You're giving your team better ingredients and nobody knows how to cook. The teams I provide outbound strategy to at AMP Social don't just collect observations from AI... they learn how to connect those observations to the buyer's world in a way that makes the outreach feel like it came from someone who actually understands. Like a real human. That's the skill... observation to connection... dots to insight... research to revenue. AI is going to make everyone faster. Business acumen is going to make your team magnetic, intuitive and consistent. Every sales team is about to have the same AI... the ones who win will be the ones who knew what to do with what the AI found. P.S. This is what we build inside Sales Team Six. Not more research. Better thinking. → https://theampsocial.com/

content: I coached an AE team that booked 5 C-suite meetings in 7 days using this Sales Nav formula that led to $200,000 in pipeline. Here's the exact setup so your team can run it. (Share this in your Slack channel) We all have done this.. you see the new exec starting.. yet you might not realize he's drowning and it's the worst time to reach out. That is why I recommend to reach out in month 4-10. I call this the Executive Transition Window. And the teams I coach at AMP Social build their entire C-suite outbound around it. Here's the Sales Nav setup: Step 1: Filter "Years in company less than 1 year" Step 2: Add your industry and seniority filters to narrow to VP and above Step 3: Target people specifically in the 4-10 month window Step 4: Save the search so fresh prospects show up daily Now once you load this up. Send them connection request that are blank for higher acceptance rates. Then if you have the right observations (product update, new hires, location expansion) send a personalized video that is less than 40-50 seconds. If no video.. send a voice note or a written message that focuses on an observation. That is the key to break through the noise.. connecting the dots. And.. one team used this exact play.... booked 5 C-level meetings in one week that led to $200,000 in pipeline for them. Sales leaders... have your team build this saved search tomorrow. content: I was running a race at full speed and my body was begging me to stop. I finally listened to it and now I have way more joy and peace than I've had in years. The story I told myself is that the grind was necessary and just push through it.. get on the red-eye flights... back to back calls. Even though I do believe there's a time and season for everything.. my body was not having it anymore. So... 7 weeks ago I made a decision that scared me... I stayed home. No travel. I committed to the following: • 20-minute morning walks • Reading my Bible daily • Weekly pickleball sessions • Going to bed around 9-10pm First week sucked not going to lie.. but then week after week it got better. To the point where I really don't feel like leaving home at all right now. But honestly this was the coolest part. My Oura ring randomly gave me a notification the other day and was like yo my brotha you went from moderate cumulative stress... to low. When I saw that... I was buzzing! Yet it was no surprise because I have felt lighter.. sharper.. and find myself diving deeper into my craft than usual as I run my business. I was convinced that being everywhere was what made me valuable. Turns out being present is what makes me valuable. And from that place of stillness... the work became the best it's ever been. I think we've been lied to about what success looks like. Everyone is out here chasing more. The thing that changed my life was doing less. P.S. When's the last time you actually stopped?

content: Outbound is starting to lose its humanity. Which has me asking... where did the humans go? I see posts say: "I scheduled 4,934 meetings in a week." Alright not that many, but you get the point. Pretty confident everyone is exhausted of this.. because it's the reality of the game we live in selling mid-market/enterprise solutions. I am seeing it real time with the clients I work with that teams are struggling. And buyers are becoming more annoyed with the AI slop fest... Somewhere along the way we believe that more messages is better outbound.. which is not the case. Sure, AI automation helps. But we have to ask ourselves would you reply to this? The best-performing teams are the ones still willing to get personal. Even now, I still send personalized messages before automating anything in my process. Every leader should have their reps get at least 100 responses manually before you even automate something. Yes you heard me 100.. I suggest teams get back to the basics: ↳ You're still messaging a human ↳ That message still has to feel like it came from one ↳ LinkedIn still rewards depth, not volume Speaking of LinkedIn, that's why I keep saying video, video, video. The teams still doing the unscalable work are the ones winning. • Doing in-person round tables • Building VIP referral programs • Sending out 1-5 personalized videos a day I promise if you track it for 30 days you will see results. Breaking into regions they had never been able to crack. The more creative you get with outbound the more you stand out. Because most teams have already given up on doing the hard stuff. content: I’m about to walk on stage. I’ve been doing this for over 10 years. And every single time… …I get nervous. I used to think the goal was to get rid of the nerves. It isn’t. Now I just say, “Hi.” Thanks for showing up. Thanks for reminding me that I care. The nerves aren’t the bad part of me. They’re one of the good parts. I don’t wait until I feel fearless. I walk on stage nervous. For the first four minutes, my heart races. My mouth is a little dry. My brain wonders if I’ve forgotten how to speak. Then something shifts. I stop thinking about myself. I start thinking about the people in the room. And that’s when the nerves take a back seat. Confidence isn’t the absence of nerves. It’s learning to make room for them. Do it nervous.

content: I bought a GE refrigerator with a warranty. Then one day the drawers decided they no longer believed in opening or closing. “No problem,” I thought. “That’s what warranties are for.” Naive. Apparently a warranty isn’t a repair program. It’s an escape room. Call this number. Now call this number. Now wait for parts. The parts arrived. Wrong parts. Schedule another technician. Earliest appointment: sometime before the next ice age. Companies obsess over making the sale. Then accidentally turn their support process into a endurance test. The product breaks once. The customer experience breaks over and over.

content: I had a discovery call I thought went great. The prospect was friendly. Lots of nodding. Lots of “yeah, exactly.” Every time they said something, I agreed. When they raised a concern, I softened. When they hesitated, I rushed in to smooth it over. The call felt pleasant. I hung up feeling good. They never bought. A few weeks later, I listened back to the recording. That’s when I heard it. I didn’t challenge a single thing they said. I didn’t offer a point of view. I was focused on being likable. I was auditioning. I was adjusting what I said based on what I thought they wanted to hear. I avoided pushing back. I edited my opinions. I prioritized harmony over honesty. That was the problem. When prospects sense you’re telling them what you think they want to hear rather than what they need to hear, they don’t trust you. Later, a mentor said something that stuck: “Buyers don’t need you to agree with them. They need you to help them think.” That was the shift. Respect didn’t come from being nice. It came from: Naming trade-offs. Challenging assumptions. Asking uncomfortable questions. Being willing to say, “That might not be the root cause of the issue.” The irony? The less I tried to be liked, the more sales I made because people trusted me. And in a world of similar products and services, trust is why people choose you. No trust. No transaction.

content: I told someone I lost my mother. They immediately told me about losing theirs. Them: “Yeah, when my mom died, I remember…” It came from a good place. But in that moment, the spotlight moved. Off my mom. Onto theirs. When that happens, the other person never gets to share what they came to share. Most people don’t do it on purpose. That’s what makes it so easy to miss. Keeping the spotlight on someone else is a skill. It takes self-awareness. Here’s a better response: “Oh gosh. I’m so sorry. What’s your fondest memory of your mom?” Now the spotlight stays where it belongs. On them. This week: Begin to notice where the spotlight is. Notice how you make other people feel when you’re curious about what they said.

content: Why people don’t buy. A homeowner notices a small crack in the wall. A handyman knocks on the door. “Want me to fix it?” The homeowner says no. Why? Not because they don’t want a better house. Maybe they’re leaving for vacation next week. Bad timing. Maybe they don’t trust the handyman. Maybe they can’t decide which handyman to use. Or maybe they’re staring at a leaking roof, a broken AC, and a kitchen remodel. Compared to those, the crack just isn’t a big enough problem. That’s how buying decisions work. When people don’t buy, it’s because: The timing isn’t right. They don’t trust you. They can’t decide on the solution. Your problem loses to a bigger one.

content: In selling, we have a strong holding-on muscle. We chase. We follow up. We circle back. But we never train the letting-go muscle. Here’s how I strengthen mine. When a prospect no-shows a meeting, I do nothing. About 70% of the time, they catch it themselves. They apologize. They reschedule. The other 30% stay quiet. I let them. I’m not chasing. I’m testing for motivation. I add them to my nurture track and move on. I don’t play catch with people who keep the ball. How strong is your letting-go muscle?

content: I had a good friend in college named Seth. He drank until he passed out. Most nights. So I did what I thought a good friend does. I lectured him. I told him about his liver. I pitched him on rehab. I gave him the full TED talk on why he had to change. He didn’t change. He just pulled away. And eventually I lost my friend. Here’s what I got wrong. I stood top of a tree, like a wise monkey shouting at a fish. “Hey Seth Fish. Get out of that pond. Come live up here in my tree.” Fish don’t do well in trees. What I wish I’d done was simple. Walk around and sat next to him in the pond. Slide him a coffee. “Morning, man. Rough one?” No judgment. No fixing. Just next to him. I can’t do that with Seth anymore. He passed away. But I carry it now. Because the moment I try to “help” someone, I’m telling them I’m one up. That I know better. Sitting beside someone and supporting them, with no judgment, is a different thing entirely. That skill made me a better salesperson. But more than that, it made me a better person. We live in a world that’s loud and divisive, where everyone is starving to feel heard. Here’s the one thing I know to be true. People don’t resist change. They resist being changed.

content: I’m giving a talk at an SKO. Mid-sentence, I notice a few people tapping away on their phones. And my brain does what brains do. “They’re bored.” “I’m losing them.” “This is officially bombing.” Cue the internal Netflix documentary: The Rise and Fall of Josh Braun. Then I caught it. And asked myself “What else might be happening?” What if they’re taking notes? What if they’re Slacking their team: “This part’s good”? What if someone’s dealing with a small fire at home that has nothing to do with me? Nothing in the room actually changed. Only the story I told myself about what I was seeing. Same phones. Completely different meaning. And almost instantly, my body followed the new story. Shoulders dropped. Voice steadied. Energy came back. Here’s the thing; The mind is like a bad sports commentator. It sees one play and immediately declares the season over. No replay. No context. Our feelings don’t come from what happens. They come from the meaning we attach to what happens. Change the story, and you change how you feel. The skill isn’t positive thinking. It’s story awareness. Catching yourself mid-narration and remembering: “This is just one possible script.” Most anxiety isn’t caused by the situation. It’s caused by the story we sprint ahead and tell ourselves about the situation. Notice the story. Don’t believe the first draft. Rewrite if needed. That’s the practice. Simple, not easy.

content: I agreed to a meeting with a salesperson. Then they confirmed it. Then they confirmed it again. Then, an hour before, they confirmed it a third time. Your boss doesn’t confirm a meeting. Your coworkers don’t confirm a meeting. The people who belong in your calendar assume they belong there. They booked the time. You said yes. Done. Confirming once, okay. Confirming three times isn’t diligence. It’s a tell. It says: “I’m not sure you actually want this, so I keep checking.” And the moment I feel you checking, I stop wanting it. Colleagues don’t confirm meetings three times. The nervous do.

content: If it sounds like there’s a fit, don’t make the prospect figure out what happens next. Sales is about leadership. Not control. Not persuasion. Leadership. If someone shares a problem you can help solve, your job is to gently and elegantly recommending a next step. Here’s what I say: “Thanks for sharing about the event. Does it make sense fo you to see slides of what’s covered in the workshop sometime in the next week or two or would you rather leave it here?” Notice what’s happening here: It’s focused on what they get. It’s specific. It’s easy to say yes to. It’s easy to say no to. That last part matters. Because I’m not trying to manufacture momentum. I’m testing motivation. People who want to solve a problem move toward it. People who don’t, won’t. That’s why “no” is a perfectly good answer. A no creates clarity. A maybe delays it. The goal of a next step isn’t to push the deal forward. It’s to find out whether the buyer wants to. “Based on what you’ve shared, the next logical step might be X. Would you be open to that, or would you rather leave it here?” They keep their autonomy. They have complete freedom to say no. And their answer tells you everything you need to know. The goal isn’t to get a yes. It’s to find out if people are motivated to buy. You don’t create motivation, you align with it. You can’t play catch with people who keep the ball.

content: Psychologist Daniel Kahneman described two modes of thinking. System 1: fast, intuitive, automatic. What’s 3x3 System 2: slow, deliberate, effortful. What’s 17x23? When you interrupt someone with a cold call, they’re not sitting around waiting to analyze a complex question. They’re answering email. Reviewing a forecast. Trying to finish a report before lunch. So the more mental effort your question requires, the more likely they are to shut down. Compare these two. System 1: “How do you currently track down receipts and purchase details when purchases are happening all over the business? Is there manual detective work involved, or does it happen automatically?” Most people can answer that immediately. Now this. System 2: “How much time does your finance team spend each month manually reconciling transactions, collecting receipts, and correcting coding errors, and what impact does that have on the efficiency of your month-end close?” To answer that, they have to stop. Estimate. Calculate. Analyze. That’s work. And people avoid work. Especially on cold calls. The goal isn’t to ask shallow questions. It’s to ask questions that are easy to answer and make people think differently. Reduce the cognitive load. You’ll have more conversations.

content: My yoga teacher said something that stuck. I told her warrior 3 was hard. She said, “Is it hard or unfamiliar?” Hard sounds permanent. Like a limit. Like something you might not be cut out for. Unfamiliar is temporary. It just means your brain hasn’t built the pathway yet. We quit a lot of things too early because we mistake unfamiliar for hard. If you relabel the experience, you change your relationship to it. “This is hard” makes you brace. “This is unfamiliar” invites practice. Same task. Different experience.

content: I wanted to know how much my friend's watch cost. But asking: "How much was that watch?" Feels invasive. Too direct. Coming in too hot. So instead I said: "I read the Panda Daytona is impossible to get. You probably paid $45,000 on the grey market." He laughed. "No. I got it from an authorized dealer for $17,000." I had my answer. Why? Because people don't like answering questions. They like correcting mistakes. That's why: "How much does your company spend on recruiting?" Gets a vague answer. But: "You're probably spending at least $500k a year on recruiters." Gets: "No, nowhere near that. More like $200k." Same information. Different path. The fastest way to the truth isn't asking for it. It's getting it wrong.

content: …….ok y’all: here’s 5 cold email tips, to close out la semaine. :) 1️⃣ More pattern interruptive punctuation (& a few acronyms), the better. 2️⃣ 5 emails to a “sequence”, over 21 business days. 3️⃣ Add a piece of written content (article, blog etc.) in email #2. & a piece of audio/video content (webinar, podcast etc.) in email #5. 4️⃣ Make sure the content is 3rd party. (Not written by you) & that focuses on solving a problem, for their specific buyer persona. 5️⃣ Pattern interrupt in the subject line. AKA look at the subject line & ask yourself, if you were the recipient…. On a scale of 1 to 10, how much would YOU be able to tell that it’s a “sales email”. If it’s over an 8, then I’d re-write it. An evergreen pattern-interruptive subject line, is to include a couple words of TRUE personalization. EX. …. you said “endless options”, Ben. The key ISN’T that you spend hours of time on the personalization— OR pick irrelevant personalization. But the key (or one of them), is that you’re SPECIFIC. That you’re saying something that they would KNOW from looking at it, that it was written to just them. One of the most common mistakes, is confusing being “pattern interruptive”, with being silly. And cracking a bunch of jokes. And the pattern they’re trying to interrupt is “being too stuffy”, or talking corporate. The pattern that I would target, ISN’T “being too serious”……. The pattern that I would target, is that the norm, statistically, is that the seller doesn’t know anything about the buyer. It’s a mass blast. Trying to cast a WIDE net. Without actually knowing anything about the prospect, they’re sending the email too. That’s what’s normal. That’s the “pattern”. So to interrupt it, you say 2-3 words in the subject line…. That are written & truly personalized, to just them. And proves to them in real time that you’ve invested in them, by doing your research. It doesn’t earn you the meeting. But it DOES earn you the right to a few more lines, to prove relevance, & find an unknown for them, that’s truly valuable. 6️⃣ I’d avoid the words in the list below…. Because they are the top-hits, of words that can cause an email deliverability problem. OK that’s it! See y’all tomorrow— Or as some would say, remain. 🙂
author_info: CEO @ Flip the Script

content: …..5 changes that I made to my cold emails, that resulted in booking 35 SQLs consistently a month, on outbound. 🙂 1️⃣ Fewer mentions of your company name, the better. If you HAVE to do it, I’d do it in the 2nd to last line of the email. 2️⃣ No same subject lines. 3️⃣ No more than 50 emails, per day. 4️⃣ Ask a question— without adjectives or adverbs, that’s anchored to their buyer persona metrics. EX. I wouldn’t use terms like—-> High, low, fast, slow, good, bad, ineffective. A buyer can’t answer a question with adjectives, & it communicates you’re not after finding an unknown problem. If I say: “do you have bad cold emails”, “do you have low open rates”, or “is your lead routing process ineffective?”…. They can’t really answer that question. What is a bad email? What is a low open rate? What is ineffective? Best case scenario, they’ll answer yo the best of their ability…. But that gives you THEIR definition of low. So you can’t use it to diagnose. FOR EXAMPLE: If I know that <50% open rate, means they have a email deliverability problem… And I ask “do you have a low open rate?” And they say no…. (or yes) I still don’t know don’t know their open rate, so I still don’t know if they have the problem. Generally those questions are used to get the buyer to “admit” they have a problem— & they know it’s a trap, so they shut down. But the WORST part, is if you’re asking “are you struggling with low open rates?” If they can say yes, it’s because they already know it. But if their open rate was bad enough, based on what they know, to do something about…. They would already have done something about it. But you’re going outbound. Which means based on what they know, it’s not bad enough to warrant action. Or it’s a fringe problem at best. But to cause a new initiative… I have to find a new problem that they weren’t aware of before— Which I can’t do, or can’t find, by asking a question with adjectives, or adverbs. So instead of asking “do you have low open rates?” I can ask: ▪️ is your open rate below 50%? OR ▪️ what is your open rate? Reason I ask is industry average is 15% - 25%, & anything below 50% means that there’s an email deliverability problem. Only 59% of cold emails on average are delivered to the primary inbox— Which means 41% of emails aren’t even reaching the prospect, & is typically a bigger driver to not hitting SQL quota…. Over email quality. 5️⃣ use TRUE personalization, in the first line of the cold email. Not name, title, or company vertical…. But something that could only have been written to them. I put the personalization buckets below. They’re ranked in order of response rate— Left to right, for the buckets. & top to bottom, for the list underneath. OK that’s it. 🙂 Peace be to journey, y’all. & good luck to all my cold emailers out there— It’s one of the hardest jobs (IMO), that you can do. So I promise, it gets easier. :)

content: Here’s 5 Cold Email tips, that I live by..…. ▪️ Fewer links, the better. 1 MAX. ▪️ 4 - 5 lines TOPS. ▪️ Don’t combo two sentences, into one paragraph. ▪️ There has to be at least one piece of (unique) value, to each email. content: Meant to say this last week, but got sick— I’m still REELING from The Sales Decade Project. Where essentially 50 of the most elite female sellers, who sold in the 80s, all got together in one room. So we could honor them, and meet them— But most importantly, so we could learn from them. Learn how they did what they did, & how they were able to make such big strides. And that room…. That day, was just electric. But for days, I couldn’t figure out why. Why it didn’t feel like a normal conference, or get together. Why the air felt so different. So I started to look for a through line. I knew about 10 honorees beforehand— And had the chance to interview about 20 of them. And even though each person & story was different, They were all… ▪️ incredibly sharp. well learned. ▪️accountable for their outcomes, not effort. ▪️ deeply technical. knew their buyer. ▪️& somehow, after all this time, they still had a deep love for sales. The reason it was different, wasn’t because it was year 1. It wasn’t because the banners had flashy colors, or because of cool swag giveaways. It was different… because they all had a love for sales, & not self. And had the same goal—> Make the field & craft of sales better tomorrow, than it is today. Learning from the past, isn’t just about learning from mistakes. It’s also about learning what’s already been done. So you don’t solve the same problem twice. & if I took away nothing else from these women— It was that learning, true learning, is a team sport. and real progress is made, only as a group. Even with ALL of their experience, when someone was presenting, the other women, weren’t in the back of the room. They weren’t on calls. Or checking email. They were in the front row… Taking notes, full attention on the speaker. After decades of experience… They didn’t have the mindset that there was nothing left to learn. They were front & center, pen & paper out, taking notes… Even after all this time. They weren’t worried about if anyone “stole their ideas”. if anything, they were worried if they DIDN’T. They all had the mindset, that great ideas are only helpful, when they’re shared. And they don’t travel very far, or have much impact.. If they’re only known by one. & that’s when it hit me. That was the difference. That’s not only what they knew— But what they embodied. They were rooting for one another, & learning from one another… And always thinking global, not local. ANY RATE, just wanted to say thank you to all of the honorees— for their time, & sharing their stories with me. And of course a massive, evergreen thank you to Lori Richardson— For including me: In your event, in your life, & for being my mentor, truly, since day 1. And Dr. Howard Dover for hosting at the UTD campus. That’s it. My heart’s officially full. pic from “after hours”, post event— Because Lori is a 24/7 powerhouse. it’s all about: We, not me, y’all. 🙂

content: In sales, if you’re struggling to know (or find) what “problem you solve” for your buyer…. The fastest way to understand a buyer, is to understand their number. & the fastest way to understand the problems they want to solve, is to find the things, that affect that number. Your buyers don’t solve problems in a vacuum. They’re trying to solve problems, in relation to the main metric that defines whether they’re winning or losing, in their role. This is Also Known As their lagging indicator metric, and it’s their final number. The one they’re measured against. And the one they’re ultimately hired, fired, promoted, or demoted to drive. And THAT’s how they define a “problem”, Is something that they need to change, that is currently affecting….. Or will affect that lagging metric in the future enough, to make it worth solving now. That’s how they determine what to prioritize. That’s how they define pain…. Is the volume they’re lacking, according to that metric. That’s how they define impact…. Is what’s happening because of the delta according to that metric. In short The driving business problem that they’re looking to solve for, ALWAYS ties back to the metric that person is accountable to…. Day in, & day out. And if TRUE discovery happens, strong discovery happens, or true value is added— That’s where it all starts.🙂 And that’s where it’s waiting to be found.

content: …Ryan Zink, CFE is one of the most elite CEOs in the game, for one fundamental reason: He knows the buyer. He is the buyer. He was the buyer. He knows what it’s like to start a franchise. & intimately knows the day to day. What to watch out for— What to consider in years 1, 2, and 10. What it takes to be successful. What can lead to …well…. Not. Simply put— His knowledge about franchising, grips further, than any polite or smooth talking conversation ever could. He’s a good leader. He pays attention to the small things. He’s detailed. And has your back. He’s a nice guy. Fun & easy to talk to— But that’s not why people trust him. They trust him because of what he knows. his level of expertise. depth of experience. And the breadth of knowledge, that he’s built and accrued over the years. They don’t trust him because he’s nice. Or fun to hang with— His customers, team, & prospective buyers trust him…. Because they should. Because they know his opinions, are pulling from decades of experience. And when he gives advice on what you should do next, it’s just….. Correct. Short clip of a convo I got to have with him live. And full episode in the comments below. But the punchline is— There is no amount of sales techniques, openers, phrases, reverses, or tricks…. That will ever outrun buyer knowledge. How you REALLY show that you care about your buyer, is learning about them. Learning what they know. Learning what they don’t. Industry averages. Statistics on what’s normal. Statistics on what’s a red flag. So when you give your advice, on a call, of what they should do, buy, deploy, prioritize, or fall by the wayside…. It’s correct. & they can trust you, because they SHOULD trust you. That’s more important than any objection handling technique, will ever be. it’s more important to the buyer… So it should be equally important to us.
author_info: CEO @ Flip the Script

content: “Hyper-personalization”….. doesn’t exist. Personalization is 1:1. EX. I saw when you said “__” Relevance is 1:many. …. Like a “cohort”. EX. Content downloads. Or CTOs. If you can send the same email to 2 peeps... Then it's relevance-based. Not personalized. There’s 4 relevance-types. ▪️ Firmographic—relative to business traits. (EX. Segment, size) ▪️ Demographic—relative to human traits. (EX. Role.) ▪️ Technographic—relative to techstack. (EX. Outreach users) & last but not least, trigger- based relevance. There’s 3 main triggers: ▪️ Inbound: Hand-raisers. (demo-requests) ▪️ Postbound: non hand-raising, marketing actions. (content downloads) ▪️ Bridgebound— Someone where an action occured, NOT re:your marketing. (buyers who changed orgs) In my view, in cold emails: “demographic relevance” (their role), is table stakes. Meaning it's centered on THEIR metrics. & finding unknowns, in regards to those metrics. EX. a CFO= gross profit margin. & solves problems regarding it. when you start to get good... is when you add in techno/firmographic. get great?? + trigger-based relevance. But when you get REAL results, is when you use personalization. In 2026, personalization isn’t a luxury. Or a unicorn that you “should do, if you can”. It’s a must. In part, because it shows respect. In part, because it shows research. & investment. In part, because it builds credibility. But mostly, because it interrupts the pattern. The pattern of 99% of emails, is 1:many... Not 1:1. Even with AI. AI can’t: — Pick the best personalization. — Or meaningful personalization. — Pick in-depth personalization. — “Hook” it, to relevance. For the "how": If you want to combo the 2 most basic ingredients (personalization + role relevance), the formula is... 1st line = personalization. (w/ a title + a specific line) 2nd= 5 word hook + a question that (potentially) diagnoses a problem, that they likely have, & likely don’t know about. NOT a “do you struggle with low open rates, & long sales cycles?” If they already know about it... But haven't moved yet: It's because it's not worth it. (based on what they know) To pump up the volume w/ a 3rd (+ trigger based)... 1st line= trigger + “but more importantly” + personalization. EX. I saw you downloaded __, but more importantly, saw your article on __. 2nd line, is same above. More below. The 1-sheeter w/ 139 triggers, at the end. + 6 personalization buckets. Bon courage a tous, out there.🙂 Don’t believe the dross that “personalization isn’t scalable”.. Or “it’s only for enterprise accounts”. Personalization, is for reps who want results. This isn’t about showing them that we know them. It's about showing them that we know something ABOUT them.. that they don’t already know. & finding a goblin, they're unaware of. 👹 Off the soap box for me. I’ll stop ranting now, promise. 🙂 But THAT’s sales. That’s the type of rep, that we should & can be. :)
author_info: CEO @ Flip the Script

content: I met 20+ Creators in person in the past 2 weeks. They all said a variation of the same thing: "We want to spend more time with other creators, in person." I spend most of my time working from home, in San Miguel de Allende, Mexico. And the opportunities to meet my creators in person are incredibly rare. In July 2025, I organized a Sales Creator Content Party, and it was the starting point of The Sales Creator Collective. And after a bit less than a year, I feel like we don't have enough opportunities for our community to meet, exchange, and learn. That's why I'm working on the first Creator Summit in Mexico City! The concept is simple: 2 days in person event in CDMX for around 100 people. Here's what I'm thinking of: Day 1: Brand Content The first day will be dedicated to creating content for brands with creators we have matched for them. We'll create long-form assets, with usage rights and distribution on the creators channels. Evening 1: Invite-only creator dinner After a busy day of recording, we’ll head to a private location to relax and discover what Mexican hospitality is all about. I’m thinking of an intimate setting with around 30 people max. The goal is to create a moment of intimate conversations so creators and selected brands can spend some time building stronger relationships. Day 2: Masterclasses Day 2 will be all about sharing knowledge in a structured setup. I’m thinking of inviting creators who are incredibly successful in their niche so they can share their experience in a structured masterclass format or a fireside chat setup. The goal is to help established creators share their knowledge with Emerging Creators who will have purchased a ticket to join the event. Evening 2: Mexico City Night Out After the masterclass event is over, we’ll throw a networking mixer and take the crowd for a crazy night out in Mexico City. I’m thinking sunset rooftop, speakeasy jazz/latino club, and after hours tacos. We’ll give recommendations for places to go for recovering the morning after 😂 What I need from my audience: - if you're a brand and want to sponsor this event, send me a DM - if you're a creator and you want to join, tell me in the comments - if you're not a creator but you'd like to purchase a ticket, tag someone you'd like to come with and I'll share an early bird code for the both of you. In terms of dates, I'm thinking late October, early November. Who's down?

content: I spent last Thursday in Barcelona with 4 sales creators. They are all saying the same thing: It takes around 10 days for their posts to reach their full impression potential. For years, LinkedIn posts were all about early traction. If you could get a ton of comments and reactions in the first 30 - 60 minutes after posting, you'd be guaranteed to have a viral post. In June 2026, this tactic doesn't work anymore. No matter how much engagement I get early on, I barely get over 2,000 impressions in the first 24 hours after I post. But if I go back 10 days later, some of my posts get 5X the impressions I got in the first 24 hours. And it's something I've seen for almost all the creators I represent. My assumption? LinkedIn is actively fighting engagement pods and completely killing early virality. And I think it's a great thing. Your content gets distributed over 10 days, and you go from a 24 hours window of opportunity to a 10 days window. What do you think? Any other explanation?

content: I've been running campaigns with 60+ brands since September 2025. Some of them were great, some of them were terrible, but most of them were in between. Not great, not bad. But a lot of missed opportunities to convert real influence from the content created by our creators into real money. Most brands start thinking about working with creators when they do a launch. They scramble to source creators, ask them for a one-off post, pay $30,000+ for the campaign and get limited results. With this budget size, it doesn't work. Instead, my honest recommendation is to spend this $30,000 over 3 months with 5 to 10 creators. You get them to post once a month to amplify your launch, and you evaluate who are the ones who qualify as brand ambassadors so you can renew them. This reduces your risk and creators would rather accept a bit less money for a longer commitment than just a one off. I did a whole presentation on that topic last week at Revenue Royale. Go check it here and tag someone who needs to see this.

content: The Sales Lies We Tell Ourselves Part 3 Believing “We lost to the competition” You didn't lose that deal to a competitor. You lost it because you weren’t multi-threaded. Your “champion”, was a cheerleader at best. Remember, champions don’t sign contracts. CEOs, CROs, Founders, this is the deal story nobody wants to tell. Your “champion” loved you. They were bought in. They were pushing internally. Until they went quiet. And you had nobody else. Bad or non-existent discovery with other departments affected by the decision. Bad or non-existent discovery with any skeptics. Bad or non-existent about competitors, pricing, and so much more. And it all comes down to No connection to the skeptics in the room. No visibility into whether the conversation was even still happening on their side. One person. One point of failure. One lost deal. Here's the uncomfortable truth: Your reps aren't losing to better products. They're losing because they're single-threaded and they don't even know it. The deals that close have three things in common: 1. Multiple stakeholders engaged 2. A clear champion with actual authority 3. Consistent activity across the account — not just one contact The deals that push or die have one: a rep who thinks a friendly contact equals a covered deal. This is exactly what Backstory.ai helps surface. Which accounts are single-threaded. Which champions have gone quiet. Which deals look fine in the CRM and are actually on fire. You can't fix what you can't see. And right now, most of you can't see it, or you simply refuse to acknowledge the issue.

content: The Sales Lies We Tell Ourselves Part 2 Believing “sales forecasts are a negotiation" Most sales forecasts aren't a number. They're a dictatorship. CEOs, CROs, Founders — you already know this. You've sat in meeting after meeting where the number magically lands at exactly what the board wants to see. That's not forecasting. That's wishful thinking with a spreadsheet. Or as I like to call it, smoking hope-i-um Here's what's actually happening: Your forecast is a dictatorship. Deals that belong in the next quarter get forced into this one. Reps feel the pressure. They say what you want to hear because if they don’t you will think they aren’t team players. You report what the board wants to see. And then you miss. Again. And again. And again. The real problem isn't that your reps can't predict. It's that nobody trusts the data enough to tell the truth. Not the reps. Not the managers. Not you. Because the second someone tells the truth about a deal slipping, they become the problem. So everyone lies. And your forecast becomes a work of fiction that everyone pretends is real until the last two weeks of the quarter. So ask yourself this: When did you last look at your forecast and actually believe it? If you even had to think about that, there’s the proof By the way, the board knows you’re lying too. If only you had the complete backstory.

content: Adam Morris has run thousands of sales searches. I asked him why CEO, founders, and CROs keep losing good sales reps. Most exec blame the reps.The real culprit is their own system. One exec kept firing reps every few months. He was sure they were the problem. The process only lived in his head. So Adam had him fix four things. Write the whole process down. Fix the handoff from sales to operations. Pull the CRM busywork off the reps. Run the exit interview and find the pattern. The turnover stopped. And he finally saw which seats were actually broken. If the system lives in your head, it walks out the door when the rep does. A system isn't a system until it's documented. Here's the kicker most leaders miss. The next time a rep leaves, look at the system before you look at the person. The gap is usually sitting in plain sight. Most of the time it is fixable in a week. Every quarter you blame the people instead of the process, the turnover compounds. Across every rep you hire. Across every manager who copies your habits. P.S. Adam Morris just joined The Revenue Vault. Watch or listen to the full interview at the links in the comments. ⬇️

content: Your SDR is trained to fail. 300 dials a day. 15 conversations. 2 meetings booked. 1 shows. That math gets the company to the board meeting. It doesn't get the SDR to a career. Three changes. 𝐎𝐧𝐞. Cut the dial target in half. Replace it with 20 hours of field work per quarter. Trade shows. Industry events. Coffee with operators. 𝐓𝐰𝐨. Pair every SDR with a senior AE on one named account. Same target. Different role. Real selling. 𝐓𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞. Hire SDRs who've never sold before. Train them on listening. Their lack of script makes them human. A team I work with switched to this in March. Meetings booked dropped 22%. Meetings held jumped 84%. Revenue per SDR doubled. Get them focused on the right work that actually drives results.

content: Every bad sales hire costs you $240K. $80K base. $60K in lost pipeline during ramp. $40K in manager time burned coaching the wrong person. $60K to recruit, onboard, and ramp the replacement. And most leaders make this hire off a resume and a 45-minute conversation. Resume walkthrough. Goal alignment. Three rounds of small talk. Nobody ever watched the rep sell. Then they hire. Month four they flame out. Then leadership says, "We have a hiring problem." You do not have a hiring problem. You have an interview problem. Here is the move. Round two, run a real play. Hand them one objection. "I want to think about it." Watch them work it live. You learn more in 90 seconds than 45 minutes of conversation. Then run a real cold call. Live. Real prospect. Your CRM. A hiring leader I coach put six finalists through this last quarter. Two could not get past hello. Three sounded scripted. One closed for next steps. He hired one. Five people walked out of the room. Five $240K mistakes he did not make. That is $1.2M he saved by running a 90-second test. The job is selling. The interview should be selling. Test the instinct, not the resume. P.S. content: Nothing fails like success. Nigel Green said it on the show. I have watched it kill more sales orgs than missed quota ever did. You hit the number three quarters in a row. You get comfortable. The same plays keep running. No new bets. No discomfort. The team stops noticing the market shifted. By the time the leader sees the slip, it is two quarters late. Comfort does not just slow you down. It chips away at the muscle that made you good. The willingness to be wrong out loud. The willingness to call a play that might fail. The willingness to fire the rep everyone likes who is not closing. That muscle atrophies fast. You do not lose it in a quarter. You lose it in a habit. So this week, find the uncomfortable call. The pricing conversation you have been ducking. The rep you should have managed out 60 days ago. The pipeline review where you stop accepting the same forecast story. That is how leaders stay sharp.

content: Pick any deal in your AE's pipeline. Ask them to name the economic buyer in five seconds. If they can't, that deal is not real. A sales leader I coach pulled up his AE's pipeline last week. 50 open deals. He couldn't name the economic buyer on 47 of them. 47 of 50 deals. Dead. A pipeline of 50 deals where 47 are unknown is not a forecast. It is a list of hopes. Here is the move. Monday morning, pull every deal over 30 days old. Run it through the POWERFUL framework. Pain. Opportunity cost. Wants and desires. Executive influence. Resources. Fear of failure. Unequivocal trust. Little things. Four of eight missing equals not real. Mark it. Move it. Or close it. Most leaders cut their list by more than half in one pass. The reset takes a week. The forecast clarity lasts a quarter. P.S. If your forecast is off by 20%+, this is the exact problem we open every Executive Snapshots working session with. Comment SESSION and I'll book a free 45-min slot.

content_signal: cold email; sales training; pipeline/building demand
content: Your champion forwards your proposal to their CFO tonight. Tomorrow morning the CFO asks two questions. "What does this cost us if we wait 12 months?" "What is the dollar return we get if we move?" If your champion can't answer both in your exact words, your deal is dead. You just don't know it yet. Most reps stop at pain discovery. They think knowing the pain is enough to sell. It isn't. Without a business case, deals stall in procurement. CFOs cut budgets. You get ghosted three weeks after the demo. Here is the 5-step business case framework that elite reps run on every deal. Step 1. The 12-month consequence question. Don't ask "what's the pain?" Ask "if this isn't fixed in 12 months, what breaks in the business?" That single shift pulls the prospect out of today's friction and into strategic risk. Strategic risk gets funded. Friction does not. Step 2. Run the RIM check. Relevance. Impact. Motive. For every answer the buyer gives you, test all three. Why now (Relevance). What breaks if we wait (Impact). Who has the motive to fight for it internally (Motive). If you can't answer all three, the deal will not survive an internal review. Step 3. Tie the pain to money. Executives don't fund friction. They fund cost. "This delay = $2M lost ARR." "Churn risk = $500K in accounts." "Manual work = 1 FTE at $85K." Back of the napkin beats no math at all. The number doesn't have to be perfect. It has to be specific. Step 4. Translate into exec language. Stop saying "improves collaboration." Start saying "ships projects 3 months faster, accelerates $10M in revenue recognition." Stop saying "better workflow." Start saying "$2M in operating costs out." Executives don't buy tools. They buy outcomes denominated in dollars and quarters. Step 5. Co-author the case with your buyer. Don't email a PDF and hope they "socialize" it. That word is sales code for "this is going to die in a Slack thread." Sit with them. Ask one question. "If you walked this into your CFO tomorrow, what would they push back on?" Then build the rebuttals together. Now it's their case, not yours. Run this self-test on every active deal in your pipeline today. Could my champion defend this deal to their CFO tomorrow morning? If the answer is no, you have work to do this week. Not next quarter. If you lead the team: pick the three biggest open deals this week. Sit with the AE for 20 minutes on each. Run the CFO test live. The deals that can't pass it are the ones slipping. Coach the gap before it costs you the number, not after. P.S. Want our help to upskill your team? Message me SESSION.

content: "It costs us a lot." Every rep hears this on discovery calls. Every rep accepts it. Every rep wonders why the deal stalls. That's a feeling. Not a number. The CFO doesn't sign feelings. Here's the move: Make the prospect do the math out loud. "How many reps are affected?" "How many hours per rep per week?" "What's the loaded rate?" Now they're solving for the number instead of running from it. A team I worked with this week got their prospect from "it costs us a lot" to "$340K a year" in 4 minutes. Same prospect approved the proposal 11 days later. Your job is helping them find their number. Don't bring one for them.

content: 73% of sales teams make at least 3 of these 5 mistakes on every discovery call. Top 20% performers hitting 120%+ quota? They fixed all 5. 𝐌𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐤𝐞 #1: 𝐃𝐢𝐯𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐧 𝐭𝐨𝐨 𝐟𝐚𝐬𝐭. You hop on the call. Skip rapport. Launch into pain questions. The prospect is jarred. Trust = zero. Fix: Reference recent company news in the first 90 seconds. 𝐌𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐤𝐞 #2: 𝐖𝐞𝐚𝐤 𝐩𝐚𝐢𝐧 𝐪𝐮𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬. "What challenges are you facing?" gets a rehearsed answer. Fix: Ask for specific examples. "Walk me through the last time this cost you a deal." Real stories surface real pain. 𝐌𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐤𝐞 #3: 𝐍𝐨𝐭 𝐪𝐮𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐟𝐲𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐜𝐭. Prospect mentions a problem. You say "got it." You just left six figures on the table. Fix: Run RIM on every pain. Relevance. Impact. Magnitude. If you sell $100K solutions, you need $1M+ of pain on the table. 𝐌𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐤𝐞 #4: 𝐒𝐞𝐥𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐘𝐎𝐔𝐑 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐜𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐢𝐧𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐚𝐝 𝐨𝐟 𝐦𝐚𝐩𝐩𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐓𝐇𝐄𝐈𝐑𝐒. Fix: Ask three questions. Who else is involved? What does your CEO care about? What happened last time you tried to buy this? 𝐌𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐤𝐞 #5: 𝐍𝐨 𝐜𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝐧𝐞𝐱𝐭 𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐩. "I'll follow up next week" = deal dies. Fix: Lock the next step on the call. A booked demo. A stakeholder intro. A mutual action plan. 3 mistakes per call. 260 calls per rep per year. 780 missed opportunities per rep, per year. If you lead the team: pull five recent discovery calls from your second-best rep. Count the mistakes. The coaching writes itself. P.S. content: Your rep just had their first call in 2 months. They opened with: "So where are we at with the proposal?" Deal died 10 minutes later. Here's what should have happened instead. I just coached a rep through this. She hadn't spoken to the prospect since August. It's October now. She was planning to pick up right where they left off. I told her to stop. Here's why deals die after time gaps. When you haven't talked to a prospect in weeks or months, everything has shifted. New priorities. New stakeholders. Budget conversations you weren't in. Internal politics you can't see. But your rep is still operating on information from 2 months ago. They show up out of sync. The prospect is thinking about different problems. Your rep is pitching yesterday's solution. Deal stalls. Deal dies. Elite reps do something different. They run a "What We Learned" call before talking next steps, proposals, or pricing. The framework is simple. Build a 3-4 section slide: → Key priority and business impact → Current state and specific pain points → Ideal state and desired outcomes → Core metrics and KPIs with quantified impact Then open the call like this: "It's been a few months. I want to make sure we're 100% aligned. Based on our last conversations, here's what we learned. Here's the challenge you're facing. Here's what's at risk if it doesn't get solved. Does this still hold true?" Let them correct you. Take notes. Adjust. You're doing two things at once. Showing them you actually listened. And giving them permission to update you on what's changed. Most sales leaders skip this step. They tell reps to "follow up" and assume that means scheduling a call. The rep sends an email, books a call, shows up cold, and wonders why the deal feels off. Your reps should never walk into a post-gap call and wing it. 20 minutes to build. Saves deals that would otherwise die. Run it before any post-gap call. P.S. content: Challenger sale is killing your team. The book was good. The interpretation got bad. Reps read it and decided to challenge the prospect. They lead with confrontation. They argue with the buyer. They mistake disagreement for insight. Here's what's actually working. The empathetic sale. Reps who feel like a peer to the buyer close more deals than reps who feel like a teacher. Three moves. 𝐎𝐧𝐞. Lead the meeting with one specific reason you wanted this conversation. Not "I'd love to learn about your business." 𝐓𝐰𝐨. Reflect what you hear before you respond. "What I'm hearing is..." gets buyers to keep talking. 𝐓𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞. When you disagree, say "tell me more" before you say what you think. The buyer wants a peer who understands the cost of doing nothing. P.S. content: He'd been on the call with me for 47 minutes. The proposal was solid. The fit was real. Then he closed his laptop and said: "We just signed a partnership that needs our full attention. Call me in October." Six months is a long time in B2B. 15 years ago I would have pushed. "What if we phased it? What if we started small?" I would have lost the relationship. I said three things instead. "That makes sense." "I'll set a reminder for October." "In the meantime, who do you know who needs the kind of work we do?" He gave me two names on the spot. We set up a 20% revenue share if either turned into business. The deferral became a referral. One of those names converted in 5 weeks. The deal he deferred is still on the books for October. Two pipeline events from one "no for now." Most reps hear "come back later" and treat it like a loss. It's not a loss until you don't pick up the phone in October. A deferral with a referral path is the second-best outcome of any sales call. When a prospect defers, ask one question. "Who else do you know who's running into this?" People who can't say yes themselves often know someone who can. The relationship is the asset. The deal is the receipt.

content: I ran a 110-person sales org that hit its number every single year. It wasn't because we had the best territory. (We didn't) It wasn't because we had the easiest quota. (We didn't) It was because we ran the same five part weekly system without exception, whether the quarter was going well or not. Most sales managers do one or two of these consistently and wonder why results fluctuate. Pillar 1: World-class onboarding. Every new rep gets a structured 30-60-90 day plan. Not a general orientation. A day-by-day map of exactly what gets learned and when. Reps who land in a structured onboarding ramp faster, retain longer, and produce earlier. Reps who land in chaos take 12 months to figure out what should have taken 60 days. Pillar 2: Weekly 1:1s. Not a status update. A coaching session tied to a real deal in their pipeline. Where are you on your commitments? What's blocking this deal? What's the next action and when does it happen? Simple form. Consistent execution. The rep who gets a real 1:1 every week improves. The rep who gets a calendar invite that becomes a check-in doesn't. Pillar 3: Weekly call reviews. Listen to your reps on calls. Recorded or live. You cannot coach what you don't hear. Most managers give feedback based on outcomes. The best managers give feedback based on behavior. That only happens if you're actually in the conversation. Pillar 4: Weekly sales meeting. Set the vision. Create the accountability cadence. Each rep shares how they performed against last week's commitments in front of the team. Peer accountability is more powerful than manager accountability. Use it. Pillar 5: Weekly training. 60 minutes of dedicated skill development. Every week. This is not optional when the quarter is busy. This is when it matters most. The teams that win consistently aren't the ones with the best reps. They're the ones where the weekly system runs regardless of what's happening in the market, the forecast, or the board meeting. When any one of these five breaks down, performance gets inconsistent and nobody can explain why. That's not a rep problem. That's a system problem. content: Promoting your top rep just cost you two. You promoted her because she crushed quota. Now you have one less top closer and a new manager who's never coached anyone. She runs the team the way she ran her deals. Direct. Impatient. Two reps quit inside six months. Nobody taught her how to coach. She got promoted on selling. Coaching wasn't on the test. Three fixes. 𝐎𝐧𝐞. Make her shadow live deal reviews for a quarter before she runs one. 𝐓𝐰𝐨. Coach her on coaching. Weekly. The same cadence she runs with her reps. 𝐓𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞. Measure her on her bottom three reps. That's the real KPI a sales manager carries. The gap most leaders miss: the management interview tests for selling. The job is coaching. P.S. content: My dad is 78. He still wears the same pants he bought 25 years ago. He came to this country from Communist China with nothing. He found work in a restaurant kitchen and stayed for years. Long past midnight, hands in dishwater, learning a whole new country one shift at a time. He never asked anyone for anything. And in all those years, I never once heard him call it a sacrifice. I figured that was just his personality. Quiet and steady. Impossible to buy a gift for. Then I had Roman. He's 9 now, and he reads the way some kids breathe. He disappears into a book and comes back an hour later with questions I can't answer. I think about that at 5am. I'm the first one up most mornings, getting the day to work before anyone else stirs. It isn't hard. I'd never call it hard. It's just the thing I do without being asked and without being thanked, so my son can spend his childhood with his nose in a book instead of his hands in a sink. One morning it landed on me. That's exactly what my dad did. The early shift. The silence. The going without, so the next kid wouldn't have to. My dad filmed all of it on a clunky Super 8. Our family. His first trip. The exact moment he laid eyes on my mom. Grainy, no sound, and more real than anything on my phone. So now I film Roman. Reading on the couch, acting out funny skits, the boring Tuesday stuff. One day that footage will be all I have, and my dad already taught me which moments matter. Happy Father's Day, 爸. I'm still learning from you.
